Tafseer:
**Proof of Tawheed from the Accepted Beliefs of the Arabs:**
The investigation into the word *Hamd* has already passed in the commentary of Surah Al-Fatihah, and it has been stated repeatedly in this book that the polytheists of Arabia believed Allah to be the Creator of the heavens, the earth, light, and darkness. Here, the Quran establishes the proof of *Tawheed* (Monotheism) upon this very accepted belief of theirs: when it is admitted that Allah is the Creator of the heavens, the earth, light, and darkness, how strange it is that these disbelievers assign others as peers and partners to God. The word *thumma* (then) can also be used to express astonishment. Here, and in the following verse, it is used in the sense of expressing astonishment. One aspect of expressing astonishment at polytheism is this: when God is the Creator of all things, where does the room for polytheism arise? The second aspect is that the apparent contradiction seen in the things of this universe—for instance, earth and sky, light and darkness, cold and heat—contains such amazing harmony for the collective purpose of this universe that no rational person can conceive that the creator and master of each of these is different. Rather, every person of insight is compelled to admit that the entire universe is moving under the will and decree of one single Controller.

*Khalaqakum min teen* (He created you from clay) intends to point toward the initial creation of man, as He said, "And He began the creation of man from clay" (As-Sajdah: 7). The life of all earthly creatures began from clay. The Quran has repeatedly stated this subject in various forms, and through it, it usually draws attention to two realities: first, to man's insignificance, that a creature created from clay should not be overly proud of his existence; second, regarding resurrection after death, that when God created man from clay and faced no difficulty in that creation, why would He be incapable of creating him again? Here, it is mentioned to clarify this second reality. Elsewhere, it is stated: "And if you are astonished, then astonishing is their saying, 'When we are dust, will we indeed be in a new creation?' Those are the ones who have disbelieved in their Lord, and those are the ones with shackles upon their necks, and those are the companions of the Fire; they will abide therein eternally" (Ar-Ra'd: 5). "As We began the first creation, We will repeat it. [That is] a promise binding upon Us. Indeed, We will do it" (Al-Anbiya: 104). "And it is He who begins creation; then He repeats it, and that is easier for Him" (Ar-Rum: 27).

*Thumma qada ajalan*. **Different Meanings of 'Ajal':** *Ajal* means a fixed term. The word *ajal* or *ajal-e-musamma* has been used in different meanings regarding individuals or nations. First, for the term of life granted to every individual by destiny; second, it is used for the Day of Resurrection, which is set by Allah for the rising of creation. Third, it is used for that fixed scale set for the destruction of a nation. The نظیر (precedent/example) for the first meaning is in the verse under discussion and also in verse 60 of this Surah. It is stated: "And it is He who takes your souls by night and knows what you have committed by day. Then He revives you therein that a specified term may be fulfilled. Then to Him will be your return; then He will inform you about what you used to do."

For the second meaning, the نظیر is the verse under discussion. *Ajal* is mentioned twice in it. One *ajal* is clearly the same one fixed as the life term of every individual; the other *ajal*, accompanied by the attribute *musamma*, is understood from the context to be the fixed term set for the rising of creation. 

For the third meaning, the نظیر is the verse: "And for every nation is a [specified] term. So when their time has come, they will not remain behind an hour, nor will they precede [it]" (Al-A'raf: 34), and other verses on this subject. This *ajal*, as we indicated, refers to the fixed scale that signals the final limit of a nation's moral decline, when the law of Allah destroys it. This scale is not like the life term of individuals, where regardless of whether one is righteous or wicked, they necessarily die upon the expiration of their term; rather, this is subject to moral laws. As long as a nation preserves its faith and character, God will sustain it, until that *ajal-e-musamma* (specified term) arrives which is fixed by God for this entire universe. One consequence of this scale being moral is that it is entirely possible that a nation's scale is reaching its final limit and its *ajal-e-musamma* is looming, but before reaching the final point, the nation may restore its right to live through repentance and reformation. Here, we suffice with an indication. God willing, we will discuss this point in detail in the commentary of Surah Nuh.

*Imtira* comes from *mari*, which means to milk, rub, or squeeze; *imtaral-laban* means he squeezed milk from the udder. From here, this word came to be used for that debate and argumentation in which a stubborn disputant tries to extract an aspect of doubt and objection even from a matter where there is no room for objection and debate.

The meaning of the verse is that it is the same God who created you from clay, and this is a truth you do not deny. Then, for everyone, He has set a term of life. It is not that the one who is born is born immortal. Then, what room for doubt is there that the God who made you from clay will raise you up from the same clay again? He has set a term for it, the knowledge of which is with Him alone. This is a clear truth that leaves no room for debate, but you seek out some way of stubborn argumentation in every matter.

That is, there are not separate gods in the earth and the heavens; rather, the same Allah is the Owner of both the heavens and the earth, and His command prevails in both. Elsewhere, it is stated: "And it is He who is [the only] deity in the heaven, and in the earth [the only] deity. And He is the Wise, the Knowing" (Az-Zukhruf: 84). The existence and survival of both the heavens and the earth testify that the will of only one God is at work within both. If separate wills were at work within them, as stated in the Quran, both would have been disrupted. This is a refutation of the polytheists' belief that while God is the Creator of the heavens and the earth, since the earth is a distant part of His kingdom, He has delegated its management and administration to His other agents. It is stated: this is not the case. The heavens and the earth are all under His direct control, and His knowledge encompasses your outward and inward and every word and deed of yours, for which reason He is not in need of any helper in this kingdom of His.

**The Mutual Relationship between Tawheed and Ma'ad (Resurrection):** 
This subject of Tawheed is an emphasis of the theme of Resurrection above. It is repeatedly clarified in the Quran that believing in Resurrection is necessary because without believing in Resurrection, this entire system remains nothing more than the game of a player, and the same applies if, along with Resurrection, one acknowledges the possibility of polytheism and false intercession. This is because if partners intercede and save their worshippers in any case—as is the polytheists' claim—regardless of what their deeds may be, then the coming or not coming of Resurrection is the same thing.

4-5: **The End of Denying the Truth and the Testimony of History:** 
That is, there is no room for denying these matters of Tawheed and Resurrection, but these people are turning away from the signs of Allah, and in doing so, they have denied the truth that has come to them from Allah; therefore, soon the news of that which they are mocking will come to them. Here, *Haqq* refers to the Quran. People were mocking the punishment that the Quran was warning about in case of denying the Messenger. It is stated: the signs of the punishment they are mocking will appear before long.

This is the testimony of history presented in support of the above claim. The meaning is: the Quraysh should not harbor the arrogance that they possess great power and glory and cannot be shaken. Before them, how many nations have passed who possessed greater power than them, and they received a much greater share of provision and grace than them, but when they denied the messengers, God destroyed them and raised other nations in their place after them. This historical reference is given here briefly. Its full detail will come in Al-A'raf, which holds the status of a counterpart (*mathani*) to this Surah.

We have clarified elsewhere that the word *sama'* (heaven/sky) is also used for clouds. The word *midraran* contains the concept of intensity, and an abundance of rain is an expression of an abundance of provision and grace. See Surah Hud, verse 52, and Surah Nuh, verse 11.

7-8: **An Evil Disposition Has Many Excuses:** 
Above, in verse 4, the mention of their turning away from the Quran served as an answer to the demands and objections they presented as excuses for this turning away. The purpose here is to comfort the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him): do not think that there is any real excuse for their turning away, which if removed, would make them believe in the Quran. No, the matter will remain where it is now. They will fabricate one excuse or another, because the reason for not believing is not that they were not shown miracles, but rather that they are not prepared to believe anything contrary to their desires.

**The Wisdom of Not Sending Angels as Messengers:** 
*Wa law ja'alnahu malakan la-ja'alnahu rajulan...* (And if We had made him an angel, We would have made him a man...). This is the answer to a third demand, which is also narrated elsewhere in the Quran. For example: "And what prevented the people from believing when guidance came to them except that they said, 'Has Allah sent a human being as a messenger?'" (Al-Isra: 94). "Then they said, 'Shall humans guide us?' So they disbelieved and turned away, and Allah had no need [of them]" (At-Taghabun: 6). The answer given is that if, by assumption, a messenger were sent as an angel, he would still necessarily have been in the shape and form of a man; thus, the same confusion would have arisen that has arisen now. The meaning is that not sending an angel as a messenger is not because it was impossible for God, but because humans can see angels only in the shape of humans and can benefit from them only in that form. So when he came in the form of a human, they raised the same objection they are raising now. *Ma yalbisoon* (they are confusing) points to the fact that it is not that this doubt actually arises, but these people are creating this doubt to lead their simple-minded followers into confusion. Regarding the attribution of *labasna* (We would have confused) to Allah, this attribution is of the same kind as *falamma zaghoo azaghallahu* (so when they deviated, Allah caused their hearts to deviate).

*Haqa yahiqu hayqan bihi* means to surround, encompass, and overwhelm.

Above, in verse 4, the threat given to the Quraysh that they are mocking a matter of truth that is inevitable and fixed, and that they will soon see the signs of the punishment they are mocking—this now points to those testimonies and examples of this reality that are present in their own history and the ruins of their land, that the messengers who came before you also warned their nations of divine punishment, and they too mocked it. Ultimately, that punishment surrounded them, and they were destroyed.

*Qul seeroo fil-ard...* (Say, "Travel through the land..."). This is an indication toward the land of Arabia itself that if you observe the conditions and ruins of your land with this insight, you will find within it many signs of the destruction of nations that denied the messengers. Here, only a brief indication is given. The detail of this summary will come in the subsequent Surah. There, the stories of the nation of Noah, 'Ad, Thamud, Madyan, the nation of Lot, etc., are narrated, from which it is known that these nations also mocked the warning of their messengers and considered this punishment a mere empty threat of which the messenger had given news. Ultimately, it appeared, and the mockers were wiped out.

**Commentary on Verses 12-13**
**A Special Style of Question and Answer:** 
Wherever the Quran asks a question and provides the answer itself without waiting for the addressee's response, reflection shows that the scope for denial by the addressee in those places is non-existent. If any belief or action of theirs is contrary to it, it is contrary to their own accepted beliefs. Precedence in the answer also expresses that, in any case, the original reality must be expressed, regardless of what stubbornness the addressee adopts in response. It should be kept in mind here that the Arabs, despite being polytheists, believed Allah to be the Creator and Master of the heavens and the earth. We have discussed this with arguments elsewhere. 

**Resurrection is the Indispensable Requirement of God's Attribute of Mercy:** 
*Kataba 'ala nafsihir-rahmah* (He has decreed upon Himself mercy). This is an expression of Allah being perfectly characterized by the attribute of mercy, and also an expression that whatever matter is a requirement of this attribute, its manifestation is certain and fixed; nothing will be able to obstruct its path.

*La-yajma'annakum ila yawmil-qiyamati la rayba feehi*—regarding the style of the Arabic in this, discussion has passed under Surah Al-Nisa, verse 87. What is to be kept in view here is that the coming of Resurrection is an indispensable requirement of God's attribute of mercy. If Resurrection does not come, it means the Creator of this universe is not the Most Merciful and Compassionate. In His sight—may Allah forbid—virtue and vice, good and evil, are identical; this is the game of a player and a dark night. Since these matters are self-evidently false and entirely contrary to the dignity of the Most Merciful and Compassionate God that He should do anything purposeless and aimless, it is necessary that He bring such a day in which His complete mercy may manifest—where He will bless His righteous servants with His boundless mercies, and the wicked and corrupt will reach their deserved recompense. From this, it automatically follows that the actual purpose of Resurrection is not to punish the criminals but to reward the righteous. The punishment of criminals is, in reality, a necessary result of the reward of the righteous. Further clarification will come under verse 53.

*Allatheena khasiroo anfusahum fahum la yu'minoon* (Those who have lost their souls—so they do not believe). The subject of this is clarified ahead in verse 31: "The losers are those who denied the meeting with Allah, until when the Hour comes upon them unexpectedly, they will say, 'Oh, how great is our regret over what we neglected concerning it,' while they bear their burdens on their backs. Unquestionably, evil is that which they bear."

*Wa lahu ma sakana fil-layli wan-nahar* (And to Him belongs what settles in the night and the day). Here, the requirement of the mention of both night and day is that an opposite verb be assumed as omitted for the word *nahar* (day). Thus, we have assumed *taharraka* (moves) as omitted and clarified it in the translation. Examples of this style have passed in other places, and excellent examples of it are coming in this same Surah.

The meaning of the verses is that if they are denying the Hereafter and recompense out of arrogance for their current worldly successes, and are mocking your Quran, then ask them: to whom belong these heavens and earth? Clearly, they belong to God, and this is a truth they themselves have no room to deny. Then tell them that God has decreed mercy upon Himself, the necessary requirement of which is that He should not create you and provide all means of your upbringing and leave you as free, uncontrolled cattle; rather, it is necessary that He drive you all toward the Day of Resurrection, in whose coming there is no doubt. So, today, those who act arrogantly, mock it, and are immersed in their worldly self-forgetfulness will beat their heads in their misery on that day, saying: Woe to us for neglecting this life for the sake of the few days of worldly luxury and preparing for our eternal ruin. Then He stated that these people should remember that whatever settles in the darkness of the night and whatever moves in the light of the day, all are in the control, power, and oversight of God. There is no scope for anything to be hidden from His sight or to budge from its place without His permission. The darkness of the night and the light of the day are both identical to Him, and He will gather everyone from everywhere, and just as His power encompasses all, His knowledge also encompasses all, for He is the Hearing and the Knowing. This subject is coming ahead with great detail in verses 59-62.

*Qul aghayrallahi attakhithu waliyyan fatiris-samawati wal-ard* (Say, "Is it other than Allah I should take as a protector, [He who is] the Creator of the heavens and the earth...?"). The second necessary consequence of this accepted truth, mentioned above, is being stated—that when everything belongs to God alone, and He is the Creator of all heavens and earth, then no matter how much you exert pressure, how is it permissible for me to take anyone else as my *wali* (protector) and *marja'* (resort) besides the God who is the Creator of the heavens and the earth?

*Huwa yut'imu wa la yut'amu* (It is He who feeds and is not fed). This is an indication toward the providence (*rububiyyah*) and nurturing of God, and also a highly subtle taunt at the idols of the polytheists. The meaning is: the one worthy of being taken as a protector and resort is He who is the Creator and Originator of the heavens and the earth, and by whose grace and bounty everyone is receiving provision—not those imaginary idols of yours, regarding whom you yourselves admit that they are not the creator or originator of anything. As for their providence, it is enough to break their facade to say that you present delicacies before them so that they are satisfied and pleased. Here, it should be kept in view that polytheists present offerings to their idols with the assumption that these are their favorite and desired foods, which they consume and take pleasure in the fragrance of. In contrast, what a God-worshipper presents in the name of God, no part of it, as clarified in the Quran, reaches God; rather, all of it reaches the needy servants of God.

*Qul innee umirtu an akoona awwala man aslama* (Say, "Indeed, I have been commanded to be the first who submits"). That is, if you claim that God has made these imaginary idols of yours partners in His divinity and commanded you to worship them, then that is your affair; I have been commanded to be the first one to submit (*aslama*) and to surrender my entire self to my Lord. So I am the one to fulfill this command; whether anyone from among you joins me or not. From this style of discourse, as we have clarified elsewhere, where the fact emerges that the prophet is the first to act upon the call he gives to the world, it also emerges that this does not create any difference in his single-mindedness and resolve whether anyone from those whom he called joined him or not.

*Wa la takoonanna minal-mushrikeen* (And do not ever be of the polytheists). This is not conjoined to *an akoona awwala man aslama*; rather, it is an independent statement. That is, tell them I have been commanded this, and you should not be among the polytheists. Although the address in such prohibitions is outwardly to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), the aspect of reprimand (*zajr*) within it is directed toward those whose conduct is under discussion.

*Qul innee akhafu in 'asaytu rabbee...* (Say, "Indeed, I fear, if I should disobey my Lord..."). That is, when I have been commanded to be the first to submit, how is it possible for me to disobey this command of my Lord? If I disobey, I fear the punishment of that horrible day which every disobedient person must face.

*Man yusraf 'anhu yawma'ithin faqad rahimahu* (He who is averted from it on that day—He has granted him mercy). That is, the true thing to fear is this horrible day and its punishment; whoever remains safe from it is, in reality, the one upon whom God has granted mercy, and the true thing to desire is the mercy of this day, because whoever is found worthy of this mercy is, in reality, the one who has attained true success. In this, there is also a subtle indication that those who consider the successes of this world as the great thing and, behind them, have forgotten the true success (*fawz-e-mubeen*) of the Hereafter, will realize their wretchedness and deprivation on that day.

*Wa in yamsaska Allahu bidurrin...* (And if Allah touches you with harm...). Although the address is in the singular, the meaning is general, and this serves as further clarification of the preceding matter. The meaning is: benefit and harm both are in the hand of God alone. If He subjects someone to any affliction, there is none who can remove it. Similarly, if He blesses someone with any good, He blesses with whatever good He wills; it is beyond anyone's power to obstruct His will. Then, what need remains to take anyone else as a protector and resort, and to pray to and beg from anyone else?

*Wa huwal-qahiru fawqa 'ibadihi* (And He is the Subduer over His servants). *Qahr* (subduing) in Arabic does not come in the meaning it holds in Urdu; rather, it comes in the meaning of having authority, control, rule, and dominance. In English, the concept of "Control" is the concept of this word in Arabic. From this comes the word *Qahhar* (Subduer), which is among the Divine Names (*Asma al-Husna*), meaning Controller. That is, the entire universe and all its servants are at every moment within His grasp and control. He is neither in need of any helper to keep them in control, nor is there any fear that when He wishes to seize them or gather them, any of them could escape His grasp. The clarification of this subject has also been made ahead: *Wa huwal-qahiru fawqa 'ibadihi wa yursilu 'alaykum hafathah...* (And He is the Subduer over His servants, and He sends over you guardian angels... until when death comes to one of you, Our messengers take him, and they do not fail [in their duties]) (Al-An'am: 61).

*Wa huwal-hakeemul-khabeer* (And He is the Wise, the Knowing). The condition for God being *Hakeem* (Wise) and *Khabeer* (Knowing) is that Resurrection be brought, because without it, this world remains aimless. In His sight, there should be no room for any intercession that can make truth falsehood and falsehood truth, because that is contrary to His wisdom and also contrary to His being *Khabeer*.

*Li-unthirakum bihi wa man balagha* (That I may warn you thereby and whomever it reaches). Usually, commentators have conjoined *man balagha* to the accusative pronoun (*dameer-e-mansoob*), meaning: this Quran was revealed to me to warn you and all those whom it reaches. But our thought is that this is conjoined to the first person pronoun, meaning: I warn you thereby, and those whom it reaches should also warn others thereby. This is a reminder of the same responsibility which is stated elsewhere: *Falawla nafara min kulli firqatin minhum ta'ifatun liyatafaqqahoo fid-deeni wa liyunthiroo qawmahum...* (Why should there not go forth from every division of them a group to obtain understanding in the religion and warn their people when they return to them?) (At-Tawbah: 122). The same truth has been clarified in Hadith as well; the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) placed the responsibility of warning and propagation upon all his Companions and stated: *Falyuballighish-shahidu al-gha'ib* (Let those present convey to those who are absent). The duty of the Ummah described in *Takoonoo shuhada'a 'alan-nasi wa yakoonar-rasoolu 'alaykum shaheeda* also supports this meaning, as we have clarified in its place.

**Decision of God is Decisive in the Matter of Tawheed and Shirk:** 
The meaning of the verse is: whether God has a partner or not, in this matter, the decisive testimony can only be God's own. He alone can tell whether He has made anyone a partner in His divinity, and if He has, then who are they and what is their nature? If He has not acknowledged any partner, then who gives you or anyone else the right to make anyone a participant in His divinity? Now come, I take God Himself as a witness between me and you for the decision of this dispute. He has revealed this Quran upon me for this very testimony, that I warn you that there is no partner of His, and whomever this reaches, they too should warn others that He is One without partner; none is His partner or associate. Now, even after this, if you are claimants that other deities are partners of God, I am not ready for this baseless testimony. My command from God is that I announce that He is the only One Deity, and I announce my disavowal of all those things which you associate as partners of God.

20-21: *Allatheena ataynahul-kitaba ya'rifoonahu kama ya'rifoona abna'ahum* (Those to whom We have given the Scripture recognize it as they recognize their [own] sons). Discussion on this segment has passed in the commentary of Al-Baqarah under verse 146. There, we explained that this refers to the righteous People of the Book. In the Quran, the mention of the good People of the Book has generally occurred in the active voice (*ma'roof*). The referent of the accusative pronoun in *ya'rifoonahu* is the Quran, whose mention passed above in *Wa uhwiya ilayya hatha al-quran*. It is also taken to mean the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), but since the context is of address to the Prophet, the third-person pronoun is not appropriate for you. In terms of the objective, there will be no difference because the Prophet and the Quran are inseparable, and inseparable in such a way that, as we will clarify elsewhere, they are substitutes for one another. We have discussed the comparison of *abna'ahum* (their sons) in Al-Baqarah—that just as a yearning father waits for his promised and awaited son, and when he arrives, the fragrance of his garment from afar becomes a harbinger of joy for him, in the same way, the righteous People of the Book were waiting for the Quran and the Prophet and were yearning for them with the same spirit, as passed under *Wa idha sami'oo ma unzila ilar-rasooli...*.

**Testimony of the Righteous People of the Book:** 
Now, to make the testimony of the Quran more reliable, it is stated that this is not any unknown testimony but a well-known one. Its mention is also present in previous heavenly scriptures. Those to whom the Book was granted before you recognize it, and those among them who are people of faith have been waiting for it and yearning for it. Only those who have ruined their souls will remain deprived of believing in it. When the testimony of God is this, then who can be more wretched, wrongdoers, and failures than those who, despite this testimony, lie against God that He made such-and-such His partner, or deny His verses when they have come to them with clear teachings of monotheism? Such wrongdoers can never be successful.

This Surah, although Meccan, is from an era when the People of the Book of Medina were not detached from this call; rather, their wicked ones were instigating its opposition, and those who were good were considering it a confirmation of the prophecies of previous scriptures. For this reason, this indication of the Quran here is very significant. Through this, there is encouragement for the righteous (*akhyar*), and at the same time, a taunt at those who are acting ignorant despite knowing. Further detail on this subject will come ahead in this same Surah under verse 114.

*Wa yawma nahshuruhum jamee'an* (And [mention] the Day when We will gather them all). The emphasis of *jamee'an* (all together) has gathered all those groups in it whose mention occurred in the preceding verses. That is, those who invented partners for God by forging lies against Him, those who denied rising after death and recompense, and those who denied the signs of Allah. Although these crimes were the shared crimes of all these polytheists, their psychological inclinations were somewhat different, which is why they are mentioned separately in the subsequent verses and each is struck according to the specific nature of their crime.

*Thumma lam takun fitnatuhum...* (Then there will be no trial [except]). Investigation into the word *fitnah* has passed elsewhere. If this is taken in the meaning in which wealth, children, the world, and worldly adornments are called *fitnah* in the Quran, then it will be prefixed to its object, and the meaning will be: the fitnah of polytheism and the false and imaginary hopes in which they are involved today—its veils will be lifted, and they will see that the supports they are living on are merely deception. At that time, they will see no way out except to lie about themselves just as they are forging lies against God today. And if this word is taken in the meaning of turning away from religion through oppression and tyranny, it will be prefixed to its subject, and the meaning will be: the fitnah in which today they are involving weak Muslims due to their denial of polytheism will cease, and they themselves will be involved in such calamities that now, when they are caught in God's grasp, they will declare their disavowal of the polytheism in whose support they made Muslims the target of oppression today, and they will lie. Whatever meaning is taken, in both cases, the tone of the discourse will be sarcastic, and the exception will be disconnected (*istithna' munqati'*). My dominant inclination is toward the first meaning, and I have kept it in view in the translation.

*Unzur kayfa kathaboo 'ala anfusihim* (Look how they lied against themselves). *Unzur* (look) is for the depiction of the scene so that a scene of the future may come before the eye of the imagination. *Kathaboo 'ala anfusihim* means they bore false witness against themselves; that is, in the world, they kept fighting and dying in support of the polytheism they were involved in, but in the end, at the very first step, they will declare its denial by swearing an oath. There is also a subtle taunt in this that in the world, they kept bearing false witness against God, as mentioned in verse 19, and in the Hereafter, they will bear false witness against themselves.

*Dalla 'anhum ma kanu yaftaroon* (But lost from them is that which they used to invent). The exact meaning of this will be that the partners and intercessors whose support they leaned on in the world by forging lies against God—all of them evaporated. None of them was of any use.

**Answer to a Question:** 
After the explanation of the parts, the objective of the verses has become clear by itself; there is no need for further detail. However, a question is possible to arise in some people's minds: some places in the Quran indicate that on the Day of Resurrection, polytheists will confess their polytheism and indeed even call upon their partners, and in the verse under discussion, it is explicitly stated that they will declare their disavowal of polytheism by swearing an oath. The answer to this question is that these conditions are stated for different stages of the Day of Resurrection. The bewilderment and anxiety the polytheists will face on that day will be such that they will see no path to salvation in it, which is why in whatever stage they see any possibility, they will attempt to create it. But: *La yubnu baytun haythu la yubna'u al-baytu* (Where a house cannot be built, it cannot be built).

25-26: *Wa ja'alna 'ala quloobihim akinnatan an yafqahoohu wa fee athanihim waqran*. *Akinnatan* is the plural of *kinn* and *kinan*. It means veil and cover. *Waqran* means burden, weight, and heaviness. Here, it refers to that weight which makes ears deaf. This veil and heaviness is clearly a spiritual/conceptual veil and weight that deprives hearts and ears of the ability to listen and understand—the very quality that is the true characteristic of humanity—and by being deprived of it, man becomes like a quadruped, or rather worse than a quadruped. This deprivation is imposed upon man as a result of his own evil deeds, for which there is a specific law. We have discussed this law, or in other words, this *Sunnatullah* (law of Allah), in detail in the commentary of Al-Baqarah. Since this occurs under a specific law established by Allah, Allah attributes it to Himself. Look at the discussion of the sealing of hearts (*Khatm-e-Quloob*) in Al-Baqarah. In this verse, the verb opposite to *an yafqahoohu* (that they may understand it), which has relevance to the word *athan* (ears), is omitted. We have opened it in the translation.

*Wa iy yaraw kulla ayatin la yu'minoo biha* (And if they see every sign, they will not believe in it). This is not just a style of exaggeration but a statement of the reality itself. That is, the reason for their not believing is not that signs and miracles did not appear before them. Finally, what is this entire universe—heaven and earth, sun, moon, rivers, mountains, clouds, air, night, day? Are these not miracles and wonders? This entire universe is full of miracles and wonders, but what is the cure for those who do not have eyes to see? They are blind to all these wonders and miracles; even if other miracles are shown to them, they will still fabricate some excuse or another and remain firm on their stubbornness. Their cure is to open the bandages of their eyes and listen to the Prophet's words and the Quran's call with the ear of the heart. They are not prepared to do this, so what benefit will miracles and wonders bring to them? *Gar na beenad ba roz-e-shapara chashm, chashma-e-aftab ra che gunah* (If the bat's eye does not see in the day, what is the fault of the sun's spring?).

*In hatha illa asateerul-awwaleen* (This is not but legends of the former peoples). *Asateer* is the plural of *usturah* and *astariyyah*, from the root *satar*. It means baseless stories, untrue tales, legends.

**The Arabs' Theory Regarding the Rise and Fall of Nations and its Refutation:** 
The history of some nations that the Quran has presented in connection with its call has been presented from that aspect which is the primary useful aspect of history. From it, this truth comes before us that the rise and fall of nations do not occur as accidental events, but moral factors play the primary role in it. When a nation becomes devoid of the moral elements of life, the law of nature destroys it and raises another nation in its place that is better than it in character and morals, and then tests it to see what attitude it adopts after attaining power. If its attitude also becomes corrupt, then it destroys it as well and brings another nation in its place. Based on this principle of rise and fall, the Quran placed the history of their own country and surroundings before the Arabs, stating that the events of the destruction of 'Ad, Thamud, Madyan, Saba', the nation of Lot, the nation of Pharaoh, etc., are not accidental events, but have occurred under that divine code set by God for the life and death of nations. These nations were involved in moral and spiritual diseases, so Allah sent spiritual and moral physicians, i.e., prophets, for their reform and cure. These prophets exerted extreme effort to cure their nations' diseases, but their nations did not lend an ear to their words, the result of which was that Allah destroyed them. By narrating this history, the Quran warned the Arabs that you, too, are facing this stage of life and death; the Messenger of God has come among you as well, and if you do not listen to his words, you too will be destroyed just as your preceding nations were destroyed. This warning of the Quran struck hard at the pride of the Arabs. First, it was burdensome for them that they are involved in some moral and spiritual disease as a result of which divine punishment is about to befall them; second, they considered it entirely contrary to reason that moral factors have any role in the rise and fall of nations. Their theory was that nations, like individuals and objects, live and die; just as an individual or a tree is born, grows, ages, then dies or dries up, similarly nations also are born, grow, and perish. This has no connection with character and faith. The statement of the Arabs which is narrated in the Quran—*Wa ma yuhlikuna illad-dahr* (And none destroys us except time/the rotation of days)—they also used to say in response to this warning of the Quran and the Prophet that it is wrong that nations are destroyed due to faith and character; faith and character have no connection with this problem. These are all miracles of the rotation of time. Obviously, when their theory was such, they had to be angry at this historical perspective of the Quran, and this anger of theirs would increase even more when they saw that many people, being influenced by this historical truth, started fearing that if they did not listen to the Prophet and the Quran, they would fall into the grasp of punishment. In this bewilderment, they would say: *In hatha illa asateerul-awwaleen* (This is nothing but legends of the former peoples)—what is this Quran, just some baseless legends of the former peoples which, God forbid, some insane person is going around reciting. Further discussion on this will come under Surah An-Nahl, verse 24.

27-28: *Faqaloo ya laytana nuraddu wa la nukaththiba bi-ayati rabbina...* (Then they will say, "Oh, would that we could be returned, and not deny the signs of our Lord..."). Here, the response to the *tamanni* (wish) is omitted (a verb meaning "so we would believe"), and *wa la nukaththiba* is conjoined to this omitted verb, which is why it has come in the form of the lightened imperfect tense (*mudari' khafeef*). Examples of this kind of omission have passed before and will come ahead. Examples are not needed to be cited, because in the response to the aforementioned wish, the coming of *fanusaddiq* or a word synonymous with it was perfectly clear, which is why it was omitted, and through the conjunction *wa* in *wa la nukaththiba*, the existence of the omitted part was indicated. The eloquence in expressing *wa la nukaththiba* is that it expresses both their regret and their confession of crime. That is, today they are acting arrogantly and treating the Quran as an old tale, but tomorrow they will regret: oh, would that we could be returned to the world to affirm the signs of our Lord and not deny them.

*Dalla 'anhum ma kanu yaftaroon* (But lost from them is that which they used to invent). The exact meaning of this will be that the partners and intercessors whose support they leaned on in the world by forging lies against God—all of them evaporated. None of them was of any use.

**Answer to a Question:** 
After the explanation of the parts, the objective of the verses has become clear by itself; there is no need for further detail. However, a question is possible to arise in some people's minds: some places in the Quran indicate that on the Day of Resurrection, polytheists will confess their polytheism and indeed even call upon their partners, and in the verse under discussion, it is explicitly stated that they will declare their disavowal of polytheism by swearing an oath. The answer to this question is that these conditions are stated for different stages of the Day of Resurrection. The bewilderment and anxiety the polytheists will face on that day will be such that they will see no path to salvation in it, which is why in whatever stage they see any possibility, they will attempt to create it. But: *La yubnu baytun haythu la yubna'u al-baytu* (Where a house cannot be built, it cannot be built).

29-32: In these verses, there is no literary or syntactic difficulty. After the deniers of the Quran, the end of the deniers of Resurrection is stated, who considered this worldly life alone to be life and were either not believers in being raised after death or considered it a highly far-fetched and impossible thing. It is stated: if you could see that time, you would see that one day they will all be raised and brought before their Lord, and they will be asked: did the resurrection and the coming of the Day of Judgment not prove to be a reality? At that time, they will say: Yes, by our Lord! It is indeed a reality. The command will be: then taste the punishment of the disbelief of this day. 

Following this, the believers are comforted alongside the warning to the deniers: those wretched ones who denied the meeting with Allah have fallen into severe loss; when the Hour of Judgment comes unexpectedly, they will beat their heads with regret and say: Woe to us for the negligence we committed in this regard by being enamored with the life of the world! It is stated that their picture on that day will be that all will be carrying the burdens of their sins on their own backs; neither will their supporters and helpers be with them, nor their partners and intercessors—the state of "each for himself." *La taziru waziratun wizra ukhra*, no soul will bear the burden of another soul. Then, while warning, it was stated: how evil will be the burden they are carrying, because this burden will not be removed from their heads ever again. 

Following this, the aspect of comfort for those who live their lives fearing the Hereafter has become more clear. It is stated: what is this worldly life that has made them indifferent to the Hereafter? Merely a fruitless amusement and play, a few days of fun and games! The actual thing is the abode of the Hereafter and its life, which is far better than this worldly life for those who fear the Hereafter. So, do you not understand?

**The Upcoming Subject (Verses 33-50):** 
**Comfort to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him):** 
The preceding collection of verses, as you have seen, ended on the theme of comfort. Now, the same theme of comfort is progressing. However, some intensity has entered this comfort, although this intensity, as far as the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the Muslims are concerned, holds highly heart-pleasing coolness within itself, because in it, the aspect of reprimand and anger is directed entirely toward those stubborn deniers who were in pursuit of the Prophet showing some miracle, that they might believe he is a prophet and that whatever he is presenting is God's word and message. Naturally, this demand of theirs used to affect the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the common Muslims as well. Especially the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), who was the embodiment of mercy and compassion and held highly deep yearning for the faith of his nation, would be affected by this demand and would become desirous that some miracle should appear—perhaps they might believe in this way. Allah first comforted the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that their demand should not be a cause of distress for you. It is not your denial but the denial of the signs of Allah; so when God is watching and listening to everything and, despite possessing the power to fulfill their demand, is not fulfilling it, then why are you distressed? The prophets who came before you were denied in the same way; they endured, and similarly, you too must endure. This is the law of Allah.

Following this, it is stated in highly sharp and intense words that if Allah wished to bring them to the path of faith by force and might, He would have made them all believers and Muslims in an instant, but He did not do so; rather, He liked that people use their intellect and understanding and choose the path of faith by their own choice and will, so why are you overcome by the desire for their faith that you become desperate for some miracle to appear that might compel them and make them bow before God? God does not want this. If you wish, bring such a miracle from wherever you can in the heavens or earth. 

Following this, it is stated what kind of people will believe, and what kind of people will keep asking for miracles. Then, the wisdom of not sending a miracle in accordance with their demand is indicated, and attention is drawn toward the countless miracles of power in this universe and the arguments and proofs Allah has stated in His Book, and it is stated that those who have closed their eyes to these things are deaf, dumb, and blind. Nothing can open their eyes. 

Following this, it is stated that if these people ask for some sign of punishment, ask them that if some punishment came or the Day of Judgment itself arrived, what provision have they made for their protection? At that time, they will only call upon God. In this connection, the history of the prophets and their nations is referred to—that We showed these nations signs of Ours in this way, but they did not benefit from them. The result was that they were destroyed. Similarly, if they fall into any outward or inward grasp, who will be there to save them except God? 

Finally, the official duty of the prophets was stated: that their task is warning and bringing glad tidings, not showing miracles and signs of punishment. And through the tongue of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), it was announced that I am neither the owner of God's treasures nor do I know the Unseen, nor do I claim to be an angel; I just follow that revelation which comes to me from God. Whoever wishes, let him believe. This truth is fixed in its place that in the sight of God, the blind and the stubborn do not hold the same rank. In this light, read the upcoming verses.

*Qad na'lamu innahu la-yahzunuka alladhee yaqooloon* (We know that it saddens you, what they say). In the commentary of Al-Baqarah, we have clarified that when *Qad* comes in this way on the imperfect tense, it indicates that here a deficient verb (*fi'l-e-naqis*) is omitted. It is as if *Qad na'lamu* is originally *Qad kunna na'lamu*, which adds the content that this matter has always been in God's knowledge, and He has never been unaware that the things these stubborn deniers say cause you grief. "What they say" refers to things of the same nature as those indicated in verse 8, or those coming in verses 37-50—that if they are messengers of God, why do they not show any miracle, why does no angel descend to proclaim their truth, why do they not show any book revealed directly from heaven between two covers, why did they not become owners of any treasure, why do they not show any sign of the punishment they are threatening us with? Etc. It is stated: we have been hearing, seeing, and knowing all these things, so despite the power and knowledge, if we did not take notice of them, then rest assured that we have understood wisdom and goodness in it.

*Fa-innahum la yukaththiboonaka wa lakin-nath-thalimeena bi-ayatillahi yaj-hadoon* (So they do not deny you, but the wrongdoers reject the verses of Allah). This is a highly heart-pleasing sentence of comfort and consolation. The meaning is that when We, despite knowledge and power, did not take notice of these absurdities, then you too endure; this is not your denial, but these wrongdoers are rejecting the verses of Allah. The meaning is that all this mockery and contempt is not aimed at you alone. The real target is Us and Our Book. Then why do you sadden your heart? Leave the matter to Us. At the same time, by using the word *thalimeen* (wrongdoers) for them, it was indicated what damage this is causing—to themselves. These arrogant and wretched people are committing wrong against their own souls. They are neither harming you nor God.

*Wa laqad kuththibat rusulun min qablika* (And certainly were messengers denied before you). To support and strengthen this theme of comfort and consolation, an indication is given toward that *Sunnatullah* (law of Allah) which Allah has liked for the testing of His prophets and their nations. That is, for the prophets and their companions to be honored with Allah's direct help, they have to pass through a long and testing phase of trial (*ibtila*). Without this, God's help does not manifest. During this time, the nations of these prophets repeatedly deny them. Every kind of suffering is inflicted upon them, and they are attempted to be tormented from every angle. In this way, whatever is within the opponents of truth also emerges, and whatever jewels are within the prophet and his companions also emerge and come to light. This is the time when the proof is completed against the deniers of truth and the prophet and his companions deserve that Allah's help be manifested for them. This is the established law of Allah, and none can change the law of Allah. The narratives of all the messengers are witnesses to this *Sunnatullah*, and you have been told a portion of these narratives so that this truth may be clarified to you that just as they had to pass through such conditions, you too must pass through the same.

*Wa in kana kabura 'alayka i'raduhum fa'inistata'ta an tabtaghiya nafaqan fil-ardi aw sullaman fis-sama'i fatatiyahum bi-ayah...* (And if their turning away is hard for you—then if you are able to seek a tunnel into the earth or a stairway into the heaven so that you might bring them a sign...). The response to the condition (*jawab-e-shart*) is omitted in this sentence, which is a well-known matter in eloquent Arabic. There are many parallels for this in the Quran. We have opened this omission in the translation.

*Fala takoonanna minal-jahileen* (So do not ever be of the ignorant). We have discussed the word *Jahl* (ignorance) elsewhere and stated that it comes as the opposite of both knowledge and *hilm* (forbearance/forgiveness); in fact, its dominant usage is as the opposite of *hilm*. For this reason, it means to be overcome by emotions. Although prophets (peace and blessings of Allah be upon them) are never overcome by the emotions of the soul and desires of the soul due to the purity of their nature, the dominance of any emotion of goodness sometimes becomes so strong within them that it exceeds the required limit. Although this is itself a highly noble thing, since the prophets are the criterion and scale, Allah does not let them exceed the required limit in this aspect as well. Understand this through an example: the prophet has a strong desire for his nation to believe so that they may be saved from the punishment of Allah. This emotion is evidence of the prophet's compassion and mercy and his zeal for the truth, but this emotion is desired only to the extent that it aligns with that law of Allah which Allah has liked in the matter of people's disbelief and faith and which was indicated in verse 34. If signs of exceeding this limit appear, Allah stops His prophet from it.

*Innama yastajeebullatheena yasma'oon* (Only those who hear will respond). *Yasma'oon* (those who hear) is in its real meaning here—that is, those who listen and understand. Those who listen but do not understand, their listening and not listening is equal. *Mawta* (dead) refers to the dead of heart and intellect, because life, in reality, is the life of intellect and heart. *Murdah dil khaak jiya karte hain* (Dead hearts hardly ever live).

**Heart-Pleasing Reprimand for the Prophet:** 
In these verses, there is a reprimand to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), but it is a very heart-pleasing reprimand, and if there is any bitterness in it, it is directed entirely toward those stubborn deniers whose faith the Prophet was so deeply yearning for that he was not ready to see any of them deprived of faith and wished that some miracle or another be shown to them so that no excuse remained for them. It is stated: if their not believing is burdening you so much, then leave them to their state; you try to search for a tunnel in the earth or a ladder in the sky and bring them a miracle according to their demand, if you are able to. We are not in favor of landing faith within hearts on the strength of miracles. We have chosen for faith a path where people use their ears, eyes, and intellect and understanding, reflect on the arguments of the horizon and the soul, listen to the Prophet's words, think and understand, and choose the path of faith by their own choice and will. If it were a matter of bringing people to the path of guidance by force and might, what a difficult task it would have been for Us? We would have made everyone a believer and Muslim in an instant. So, do not become overwhelmed by the desire for their faith and lose sight of the wisdom and law of Allah in this matter. If you wish, then bring such a miracle. God does not want this. If you are overcome by such emotions, then do not lose sight of what God has set as the law and wisdom for such matters. After this, it is stated that only those will accept your call who are listeners and understanders and whose hearts are alive; those who have become deaf and dumb and whose hearts have died, leave them to Allah; when they are raised on the Day of Judgment, all truths will be revealed to them. Do not trouble yourself with grief behind them.

*Wa qaloo lawla nuzzila 'alayhi ayatun mir-rabbihi* (And they say, "Why has a sign not been sent down to him from his Lord?"). This could refer to any of the signs demanded by the disbelievers, but the context indicates that here those people are being answered who were demanding some sign of punishment that would prove that if they denied the Prophet, punishment would befall them. From the subsequent verses, this context will itself emerge clearly, which is why there is no need to mention arguments. In this Surah and in other places of the Quran, this demand of the disbelievers from their respective prophets is narrated: if you are true in your claim that we denied you and punishment should come upon us, then show us some sample of this punishment that you are warning about; this will decide the dispute between us and you.

*Wa ma min dabbatin fil-ardi wa la ta'irin yatiru bijanahayhi illa umamun amthalukum* (And there is no creature on [or within] the earth or bird that flies with its wings except [that they are] communities like you). In this segment, the same principle of omission applies as in Surah An-Nisa, verse 134. That is, in one part of the sentence, some words are omitted, as the mentioned part becomes proof of the omitted part itself. For example, in the first part of the sentence, there is the word *fil-ard* (on the earth), so in the second part, the word *fis-sama'* (in the sky) has been omitted. Similarly, in the second part, there are the words *yatiru bijanahayhi* (flies with its wings), so in the first part, the words *tadibbu 'ala rijlayha* (walks on its legs) or *'ala arjulihim* (on their legs) are omitted. In eloquent Arabic, such an omission is a well-known thing. In the Quran, there are many parallels for this. In such places, it is better to open the omitted part; otherwise, the entire meaning remains incomplete or the structure of the sentence does not stay right. Thus, we have opened it in the translation.

**Indication Toward the Signs of the Horizon:** 
*Illa umamun amthalukum* (except [that they are] communities like you)—the words are apparently very brief, but if you reflect, there is great detail hidden within them. Those who ask for signs are directed toward the signs of the horizon: you ask for one sign? The entire universe of God is filled with signs. Every creature walking on the earth and every bird flying in the space is a sign of God. If you reflect, you can see that just as there are countless signs of God's power, wisdom, and providence within your individual and collective existence, which are witnesses to you of *Tawheed*, recompense, and the truthfulness of the prophet's call, similarly, within other creatures of this universe as well, there are arguments of the Creator's power, wisdom, and providence. Just as you are a species, they are also separate species; just as you possess a nature, they also, in terms of their instinctive demands and purpose of creation, possess consciousness, perception, and emotions within them. Just as your collective consciousness has prompted you to bind yourselves within a political system, similarly, within every species of theirs is also a consciousness of their collective existence which prompts them—like the parts of a single unit—to arrange for the survival and protection of their collective existence and to perform their part in the completion of the purpose of their species' creation. In Surah An-Nahl, the Quran has cited the example of the honeybee in proof of this very truth to which there is an indication here, and pointed toward the signs of the Creator's power, wisdom, and providence that are manifest in its species-system. Today, science has lifted veils from the instinctive characteristics and wonders of various species of animals; although each of these discoveries is sufficient to amaze a human, look merely at the honeybee and the ant among the very insignificant and small things, and observe them—your intellect will be stunned. What a system of offspring-raising they have, what alertness for protection against dangers, what foresight to cope with future conditions, what intense feeling of collective duties! What high division of labor, what a strong system of command and obedience, what tireless activity for the provision of needs. What a mastery of construction for the protection of their homes and reserves, what a deep passion for seeking and searching, what degree of cleverness and sharpness for the attainment of the goal, and how much self-sacrifice and devotion.

Now the question is: has all this come into existence by itself and is it running on its own, or is there a Creator and Manager behind it? If there is a Creator and Manager behind it, has He sat down indifferently in a corner after creating everything, or is He protecting this entire universe directly? Is all this a manifestation of some blind, deaf, dominating force, or is it a bounty of the power and mercy of an All-Powerful, Eternal, All-Knowing, All-Wise, and Most Merciful and Compassionate Being? Is it a wrestling ground for different wills, opposite forces, and countless goddesses and gods, or is it a manifestation of the power and wisdom and mercy and providence of one single God, One without partner? Does it appear entirely aimless, purposeless, and without an end, or is everything within it calling out and testifying that there is a great objective behind it whose manifestation is certain and definite? Does it testify from its outward and inward that man has been left as free, uncontrolled cattle to eat, drink, indulge, and one day perish, or does it testify that the Almighty who made this world, the Wise who has shown the glory of His wisdom in each and every particle of it, the Merciful who has spread these banquets of grace and providence, will surely gather everyone one day and will surely weigh every good and evil and then deal with them accordingly? 

If human nature has not been distorted and his intellect paralyzed, he is compelled to admit that the second statement is correct. If this is correct, and self-evidently it is, then the Quran is calling to believe in this very thing. Then what need is there for any miracle to believe in it? Every particle of this universe is a witness to this truth. If man has closed his eyes to the testimonies within his own existence, he should open his eyes and look at these signs on the horizon, which are present below and above.

*Ma farratna fil-kitabi min shay'in thumma ila rabbihim yuhsharoon*. Usually, people have taken "the Book" to refer to the divine register in which everything is recorded. Although there is room for this meaning, in the light of the context, our mind repeatedly goes toward the fact that here "the Book" refers to the Holy Quran. The meaning is: just as this universe is filled with those signs that testify to the truthfulness of the Prophet's call, similarly We have not left any stone unturned in stating Our arguments and proofs in the Quran. One argument after another has been proven with such irrefutable arguments, and proof has been established from so many different styles and angles that only the blind and deaf of intellect and heart can be incapable of understanding them. Along with this, in the words *Thumma ila rabbihim yuhsharoon*, there is a subtle taunt that as far as signs and arguments are concerned, this scripture of the universe is full of them, and this scripture of the Quran is also full of them, but those who do not wish to understand anything—what is their cure? They will open their eyes and believe only when they are presented before their Lord.

*Wallatheena kaththaboo bi-ayatina summun wa bukmun fiz-zulumat*. In *bi-ayatina*, "signs" refers to the verses of the Holy Quran. The meaning is that those who are denying the Quran are like those dumb and deaf people who, along with being dumb and deaf, are also surrounded by darkness—they can neither listen nor call out to anyone, nor can they answer the call of one calling out, nor see the signs of anyone, nor obtain guidance from any sign. What is the cure for such blind and deaf people?

*Man yasha'illahu yudlilhu...*—as we have clarified in various places, this points to that *Sunnatullah* under which someone obtains the grace of guidance and someone becomes deserving of misguidance. From the preceding verses, it has become clear that those who do not open their eyes and ears, God does not force His guidance upon them. The grace of God supports only those who themselves intend to walk the path of truth and use the abilities God has granted them.

40-41: *A-ra'aytum wa a-ra'aytakum*. The occasion of use and meaning are the same. Thus, ahead in verse 46, *a-ra'aytum* is used in exactly the same occasion. 

**Another Psychological Argument for Tawheed:** This is also an answer to the demand for a sign of punishment and also an irrefutable psychological argument for Tawheed from a specific aspect of human nature. The meaning is: tell them that with this pompousness with which you are demanding punishment, what provision have you made to counter it or escape from it? What invincible defensive line have you built that you are challenging God with this arrogance? Assume: God sends the very punishment you are demanding, or the Day of Judgment you are warned of arrives—then who is there whom you will call upon? Is there any other than God whom you will call upon if you are true in your claim that He has other partners? It is stated: this claim of yours is entirely baseless. If such a time arrives, you will forget everyone except God and will call upon Him alone, and He is the One who will remove this disaster if He wills. 

In the preceding verses, as we stated, there was an indication toward the cosmic arguments for Tawheed. Here, there is an indication toward the psychological argument for Tawheed which is stated in the Quran in various styles. The fundamental point of this argument is that since the testimony of one single God is already present within human nature, the other idols and deities he believes in possess no testimony in the depth of his nature; rather, they are merely imaginary, which is why when his life reaches a stage that is the real test, man forgets all these artificial supports of his and calls upon only that God whose testimony he finds within his nature. The Quran has clarified this truth in highly heart-illuminating parables and highly truth-revealing arguments. All these things are coming under discussion ahead, which is why we suffice with an indication here.

42-43: *Wa laqad arsalna ila umamin...* (And We have already sent to nations...). *Tadarru'* means humbleness, that is, bowing toward someone with submission, awe, and a sense of helplessness and indigence. Here, this word is used for bowing toward God with the heart's fear and the sense of servitude and turning to Him (*inabah*). The opposite word is *qasawat* (hardness), which is the Quran's known word for expressing the heart's hardness, fearlessness, and arrogance.

**The Law of Allah Regarding Sending Punishment Upon a Nation:** 
Now, this is a *Sunnatullah* (law of Allah) stated, through which, on one hand, it is intended to clarify the method He adopts in sending punishment upon a nation to which He sends His messenger, and on the other hand, to bring to light this historical testimony that whichever nation made the condition of faith to be a sign of punishment, did not attain the blessing of faith, but ultimately its root was cut only through punishment. It is stated: before you (the address is to the Prophet, peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), We sent messengers to many nations, so We involved them in various kinds of financial and physical calamities so that fear of God might be born in their hearts and they might think that if they did not listen to the prophet, ultimately the decisive grasp of God would befall them and they would be destroyed. But observe, history is a witness, that instead of their hearts softening somewhat due to these divine warnings, they became even harder, and the evil deeds they were involved in, Satan made them appear so attractive in their sight that instead of refraining from them, they became even more bold in them.

*Falamma nasoo ma thukkiroo bihi...* (So when they forgot that by which they had been reminded...). *Ma thukkiroo bihi* will mean "that by which they were reminded." Here, it refers to the same *ba'sa'a* and *darra'a* (adversity and distress) in which Allah involved them so that they might understand that if they did not listen to the prophet, then after these warnings, the stage of decisive punishment lay ahead. But instead of taking a lesson from it, they ignored them after they passed, as if such incidents occur in the life of nations anyway; which nation has not faced famine, floods, storms, epidemics, and diseases? *Qad massa aba'ana ad-darra'u was-sarra'u* (Adversity and ease touched our fathers as well). Such soft and harsh conditions have already been faced by our predecessors. Then why assume that this is the result of denying someone?

*Fatahna 'alayhim abwaba kulli shay'in hatta itha farihoo bima ootoo akhathnahum baghtatan* (So when they forgot that by which they had been reminded, We opened to them the doors of every [good] thing until, when they rejoiced in that which they were given, We seized them suddenly). Now, the next stage of the aforementioned *Sunnatullah* is being stated, which is referred to in other places in the Quran as *Imhal* (granting respite). The meaning is: when the deniers of the messenger ignore the warnings of God, God grants them respite, the doors of all their desires are opened for them, until they become obsessed, arrogant, and haughty with their successes. This time is the time for their cup to overflow. At that time, God seizes them suddenly, and then they are left entirely in despair and bewilderment. The word *ublisa* has been discussed elsewhere.

**When is the Root of a Nation Cut?** 
*Dabar* means root or foundation. The meaning is: when the deniers of the messenger reach this limit of drunken revelry, then God cuts the root of such wrongdoers. In this, there is a highly subtle indication that before this, the trials that befall such criminals affect only the branches and fruits of their tree of existence, and that too temporarily; their root remains protected. But when this time arrives, God sends upon them punishment that uproots their national existence entirely from the root, because the tree that has become poisonous and is now yielding only poisonous fruit, its remaining is contrary to the benefit of this world, which its Most Merciful and Compassionate Creator and Master created *bil-haqq* (with truth). He is the Lord of the worlds. How can the Lord tolerate leaving such a harmful tree to occupy space in His garden, whose poisonous air and whose poisoned leaves and fruit are ruining the entire garden? Therefore, worthy of praise and gratitude is that Lord of the worlds who cuts the root of such wretched nations. 

Through the explanation of the parts, the order and objective of the verses have become clear; the remaining detail has not come. But the historical testimony to the *Sunnatullah* referred to here is only indicated, it has not been detailed. This detail will come in the next Surah, Al-A'raf, which holds the status of a counterpart (*mathani*) to this Surah. In it, the history of the chastised nations of Arabia is narrated, from which this truth is fully clarified that these nations also demanded signs of punishment from their prophets, thus Allah involved them in various financial and physical trials to soften their hearts and turn them toward Him, but they, instead of taking a lesson from them, attributed them to accidental incidents and ignored them entirely. After this rebellion of theirs, God gave them complete respite. They saw success after success in every path. Satan taught them that the path you are walking on is the path of success—well done, keep advancing. Ultimately, when they saw green everywhere and the intoxication of success made them drunk, suddenly God's punishment surrounded them, and all their intoxication vanished. The meaning is that this very stage is also facing those who deny you and demand signs of punishment from you. If they do not take a lesson from the conditions, they too will repeat the same history. Stay satisfied.

*Qul ara'aytum in akhathallahu...* (*Sadafa, yasdufu, sadafan* means to turn away or deviate from something and to stop from it). 

**Another Aspect of Answering the Demand for Punishment:** 
This is another answer to the same demand for punishment from another aspect. Above, it was stated that if God sends any earthly or heavenly calamity upon you, there is none to hold His hand. Similarly, it is stated here that if God involves you in some intellectual, inward, and spiritual punishment, then there is none to save you. Right now, your faculties of hearing, seeing, and understanding are alive, but you are not using them. If God snatches away your *sam'a* (hearing) and *basar* (sight) and puts a seal upon your hearts, then tell me, who is there other than Allah who can restore these abilities and faculties of yours? Then it is stated: these people are asking for a sign. See how We are presenting Our signs from how many different angles, yet they are persistently turning away instead of listening and understanding.

*Qul ara'aytakum in atakum 'athabullahi baghtatan aw jahratan...* (Say, "Have you considered: if the punishment of Allah should come to you suddenly or openly...?"). *Baghtatan* means suddenly, unexpectedly, without any notice, in total unawareness; *jahratan* means openly, with a blast, in broad daylight. The meaning is: they are demanding a sign of punishment; ask them: whether the punishment of God comes secretly or openly in broad daylight. First, who will stop it? Then, ask them: if this lightning strikes, whom will it strike? Will it strike these same wrongdoers who are inviting it with their wretchedness and who deserve it due to their evil deeds, or someone else?

**The Law of Allah Regarding Decisive Punishment:** 
It should be kept in view here that this refers to the punishment that comes upon nations denying messengers after the completion of proof. Regarding this punishment, the *Sunnatullah* is that those who deny the prophet are destroyed and the believers are saved. As for the trials and sufferings that come for warning and reminder, whose mention passed above with the words *ba'sa'a* and *darra'a*, they do not come with this distinction; rather, all receive a share in them. However, the believers benefit from them, and the disbelievers remain firm on their mischief as before—in fact, as passed above, they become even more stubborn due to them.

48-49: **The Original Objective of the Mission of Messengers:** 
Above, in relation to the same demand, the original purpose of the mission of messengers is clarified: they are not sent to show signs of punishment, or to bring punishment, or to display wonders and miracles, but they are sent to bring glad tidings of God's mercy and, in the case of denial and disobedience, to warn of His punishment. The glad tidings are clarified as: *Faman amana wa aslaha fala khawfun 'alayhim wa la hum yahzanoon* (So whoever believes and reforms, no fear will there be concerning them, nor will they grieve), and the warning is detailed as: *Wallatheena kaththaboo bi-ayatina yamassuhumul-'athabu bima kanu yafsuqoon* (But the ones who denied Our verses—the punishment will touch them for their disobedience). 

From this, one truth was clarified: Allah's messengers are manifestations of God's mercy; punishment is not among the purposes of their mission but among the concomitants and results of their denial. Second, from messengers, people should desire that for which they come—that is, guidance to faith and righteous deeds—not the thing from which they are sent to save people. Third, these wonders and miracles are neither among the characteristics of the messengers nor among the concomitants of their teaching and call; rather, their appearance, if it occurs, is merely by way of completing the proof, if Allah wills. Fourth, in this, there is a message of comfort for the Messenger: that he should keep his connection with his original purpose of mission—glad tidings and warning—and leave the matters unrelated to his duties to God, so that he does not become distressed over them needlessly.

**The Decisive Announcement:** 
Now, through the tongue of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), a final and decisive announcement has been made on this matter: if you want to dispute, then do so over that which I am presenting before you and of which I am the caller. Why do you dispute over those things that I have never even claimed? If I do not possess treasures, then when did I claim that I possess treasures? If I cannot tell when the punishment will come upon you or when the Day of Judgment will occur, then when did I claim that I know the Unseen? If I am not an angel, then when did it come from my tongue that I am an angel? I only follow the revelation that comes to me from God, and I only invite you to that. Finally, it was stated: ask them that if they are even a little of the thinking and understanding kind, then tell them: will the blind and the seeing—that is, those who walk with their eyes open and those who walk blindly following their desires—ever become equal in the sight of God? Will those who use their intellect and insight and those who tie a bandage over their intellects and live their lives ever become equal? Will the reward and punishment of the righteous and the wicked ever be the same? If the answer is in the negative, and self-evidently it is, then the same self-evident truth is being presented before you by that revelation which I am inviting to, so what meaning does your opposition hold that I do not show you any sign of punishment? If I do not show you any sign of punishment today, does that invalidate the law of reward and punishment which I am proclaiming? O servants of God, you do not reflect on this.

**The Upcoming Subject (Verses 51 to 55):** 
Ahead, it is stated that those who are seekers and waiters for signs and punishment, leave them (the address is to the Prophet, peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to their state, but warn those who hold the fear of God and fear of the Hereafter through this Book which is being revealed upon you. Then, it is stated that the true rightful ones of your compassion, training, and your attention and grace are these poor Muslims who are seekers of God's pleasure and whom the quest for religion has gathered around you. As for those arrogant and rebellious people who keep asking you for signs and miracles and walking arrogantly, neither pay heed to them, nor ignore the rights of the true rightful ones behind them, nor cast away these poor Muslims from yourself at their saying. These people consider it their disgrace to sit beside these poor ones in your gathering or listen to your words. Their desire is that if you wish to draw them near to yourself, then first cast away those who are not their equals and whose presence is a humiliation for them. Do not encourage this arrogance of theirs in the least. If they remain deprived of faith due to this arrogance of theirs, you bear no responsibility for it. They will not be held accountable before God on your behalf for this. Similarly, the responsibility that is upon you, none of them will become a bearer of it—that by casting away the believers at their demand, you take the responsibility of a great wrong upon your head. Then, it is stated that for these arrogant ones, their wealth, position, and lineage have become a veil. They think that since all the supremacy of the world is their share, then if this new religion held any aspect of goodness, could God have found only these people for it—who are not even granted two meals a day and proper, whole clothes? These wrongdoers do not know that God gives the baubles of this world to both the worthy and the unworthy, but the invaluable wealth of religion is attained only by His grateful servants. After this, the hearts of these poor Muslims were consoled so that if they were heartbroken by the taunts of these arrogant ones, it might be healed. For this consolation, the greeting and message that have come from the Lord of the Worlds in the name of these poor ones hold within every letter the glad tidings of eternal life and the honor of controlling the universe. In this light, read the upcoming verses.

**Instruction to the Prophet to Turn Toward the People of Fear:** 
From the verse above, it has become clear to whom the Prophet should be occupied with and to whom he should be indifferent. Now, this is being clarified further: do not waste your time behind those wretched people who are intoxicated in their pride of wealth and position and do not give you any importance because you are not the owner of treasures. Do not be indifferent to the rights of the people who have believed in the signs of Allah and are engaged in His worship and obedience morning and evening. It should be remembered that this instruction was not given to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) because it was feared he might ignore his companions or remove them from his presence; rather, it is a blow struck at the pride of the leaders of Quraysh, but instead of saying it by addressing them, it is said by addressing the Prophet so that it may become clear to them that their talk is not even worth responding to by addressing them directly. The meaning is: let these people say whatever rubbish they want. If this is an obstacle in their advancing toward Islam, then let them fall into whatever Hell they wish; you bear no responsibility for them. Now, accountability is to be taken from them.
