They have no responsibility for your reckoning, and if you have ignored these servants of Allah behind them, they will not become responsible on your behalf to take the responsibility of a great wrong upon your head by casting away those who are the true rightful ones of your attention and compassion. The word *tard* (to cast away) used here is certainly hard in its meaning, because it means to shove away and cast away, but this word has been used while keeping the mentality of the leaders of Quraysh in view, because their desire was precisely that Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) cast these people away from himself, and then we will become ready to talk.

**A Special Aspect of the Character of Prophets:** 
This aspect of the character of the prophets (peace and blessings of Allah be upon them) should also be kept in view here: they are, as is stated in the Quran, extremely eager for the faith of their nation. Prophet Jesus has also stated a parable on this matter. He said that is it not that when one of you loses a sheep, he wanders in distress in streams, valleys, and forests in its search and forgets his original flock? Then, when it is found, he brings it home on his shoulder and says to his people: "Rejoice with me, for I have found my sheep which was lost." This parable manifests the same compassion that Allah has for His servants who turn toward guidance after misguidance, and similarly manifests the restlessness of the prophets for the faith and reform of the straying ones of their nation. This quality of the prophets is a beloved and liked quality, which is a reflection of the attribute of Allah. However, this quality also keeps some limits and restrictions. Allah does not like that the prophet wanders in search of lost sheep so much that he becomes negligent in the care of his original flock. The rebellious and straying sheep that does not come under control is the share of some wolf. Similarly, it is stated here: if these arrogant people desire their flattery to the extent that you remove those from your presence who are engaged in the quest for God's pleasure morning and evening, then you are not authorized to do this. Your responsibilities are upon you.

In *Liyaqooloo*, the letter *lam* is *lam-e-'aqibat* (of consequence), which does not express cause but the result.

**Poverty and Riches in This World are for Testing:** 
This is an established law of Allah that if We have given someone wealth, it is not because he deserved it. Similarly, if someone is given poverty, it is not because he deserved it; rather, We have tested both by giving wealth to one and poverty to another. He gives wealth and position to whom He gives so that He may see whether they become grateful, humble, and obedient servants of His by obtaining the blessed favor of Allah, or become arrogant and haughty, becoming those who show off, cast away the poor, and sit as monopolists of divine blessings. Similarly, to whom He gives poverty, He gives so that He may see whether they remain patient in their poverty, contented with attained blessings and their own bread, satisfied with their destiny, and self-respecting in their poverty, or become hopeless and broken-hearted, low-spirited, spiritless, complaining of destiny, angry with God, and become wretched and humiliated. It is stated: We have placed them in this test. We granted them from Our favor so that they might become Our grateful servants. But due to their lack of understanding, Our favor became a fitnah for them. They presumed that they alone are the birthright owners of all blessings and the ancestral monopolists of all honor and position. The result of this lack of understanding was that now they began saying: if this Islam held any merit, would God have found only these low-class, wretched, and destitute people to honor with it? After all, where did we, the nobles, the leaders of Quraysh, and the chiefs of Ta'if die, that this blessing descended and it could not find our address and descended upon them? The Quran has referred to this pride of the leaders of Quraysh in other places as well; for example: *Wa qalal-latheena kafaroo law kana khayran ma sabaqoona ilayhi* (And those who disbelieved said about the believers, "If it had been a good thing, they would not have preceded us to it") (Al-Ahqaf: 11). Elsewhere: *Wa la aqoolu lillatheena tazdaree a'yunukum lan yu'tiyahumullahu khayran* (And I do not say to those whom your eyes hold in contempt, "Never will Allah give them any good") (Hud: 31). Faith and Islam aside, they considered even prophethood their monopoly and openly said that if God were to make someone a prophet, He would have made some chief of Mecca or Ta'if, for this office, only these (Muhammad, peace be upon him) were found.

**The True Recipients of the Blessing of Religion:** 
In response to this rebellion of theirs, the Quran stated: *Alaysallahu bi-a'lama bish-shakireen* (Is Allah not most knowing of those who are grateful?). The meaning is that the religion of God is not gold and silver, silk and velvet, whose saddles and trappings can be seen even on donkeys and mules, horses and camels. This is a heavenly blessing and divine mercy which is only the share of those who remained grateful to their Lord in every condition, those who appreciated the blessings of God, those who fulfilled the right of their abilities, those who kept their ears open, those who did not bandage their eyes with pride, and those who did not let their hearts die. Those wretched and ungrateful people who used all the outward and inward blessings of God against God Himself have no share in this blessing. The world is attained by both the good and the bad, but the blessing of religion is attained only by those who are grateful to God.

*Salamun 'alaykum* is a greeting, just as it is a word for parting; it is also a word for welcome. For example, angels, while welcoming those who remain steadfast on monotheism, will say: *Salamun 'alaykum udkhulul-jannata bima kuntum ta'maloon* (Peace be upon you, enter Paradise because of what you used to do).

**Instruction to Welcome the Poor Muslims:** 
Now, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is instructed to adopt an attitude exactly opposite to that which the leaders of Quraysh demanded. The leaders of Quraysh, as stated, wanted Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to shove away these people from himself, but Allah, in contrast, instructed the Prophet that when these servants of Ours who believe in Our signs come to you, you welcome them with the prayer of peace and mercy, and bring them the glad tidings on Our behalf that Allah has made mercy upon Himself for His faithful servants. He will surely bless you with His mercy. If any mistake is committed by any of you out of ignorance and then he repents and reforms, then Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.

**The True Yearning of the Believers:** 
Here, the glad tidings that Allah has given are only of mercy and forgiveness. There is no trace of any worldly wealth and position along with it. This sheds light on the inner self of those poor but world-and-world-possessions-indifferent servants of Allah. It shows that despite being deprived of worldly means, they held no longing for this world in their hearts. If they had a longing in their hearts, it was that they attain the pleasure of their Creator and Master. Allah blessed them with the very thing they were most desirous of, in accordance with this inward longing of theirs. From this, it is also indicated that the things for which the worshippers of the world die, in the eyes of the faithful servants of God, they do not have the value of a gnat's wing, neither in this world nor in the Hereafter.

*Wa litastabina...* (And so that...). The conjoined clause is omitted here. If the entire matter were opened, it would be: *Wa kathalika nufassilul-ayati litastabina sabeelul-mu'mineena wa litastabina sabeelul-mujrimeen*. Examples of this kind of omission have passed. It was explained above in detail what the way of those who do not wish to believe is and what the way of those who believe is. Finally, it is indicated that this entire detail We have narrated so that the way of both and the way of thinking of both may become clear to you, so that nothing becomes a cause of distress without reason. 

**The Upcoming Subject (Verses 56 to 67):** 
Ahead, the subject that has been running from above is stated in new styles and from new aspects. First, it is announced through the tongue of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that you assign partners to God and fight with me in support of this polytheism, but my Lord has forbidden me from the worship of these partners. For this reason, there is no possibility for me to follow your innovations. This fact that God has forbidden me from this thing is based on a clear argument and decisive proof. In this chapter, the testimony of my Lord is present in the form of that revelation which comes to me, but you deny this revelation and are not prepared to affirm it until you are shown the punishment of God. This thing is not in my power; the power of it is with God alone. I cannot say anything except that my Lord will decide this dispute, but when and in what manner? The answer to that is only with Him who is the knower of all the secrets and mysteries of His universe. 

Following this, it is stated that the fact that one has to rise after death, then after rising, appear before one true God, and then everyone has to account before their true Master—this is a truth whose rehearsal is happening every day on the stage of this world, but what is the cure for those who have closed their eyes!

Following this, attention is drawn toward this psychological disease of man that when he is placed in some trial, he cries out "God! God!" and forgets everything besides Him, but when God saves him from that trial, he loses himself again in his previous intoxications and follies, as if nothing ever happened at all, and he begins to think that now he is entirely outside the grasp of God. The meaning is that these people who are demanding punishment today are involved in the same disease. Tell them that for everything, there is a fixed time with God. When that fixed time arrives, you will see it yourself. In this light, read the upcoming verses.

**The Common Occasion of the Use of 'Qul':** 
The word *Qul* (Say) is in this verse, and it has appeared repeatedly in subsequent verses as well. This is evidence that all these things have been said through the tongue of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) in response to the doubts, objections, questions, and demands which were presented by the disbelievers in the heat of the dispute at that time. To understand them well, it is necessary to keep their background in view, because this clarification is not present everywhere in the Quran as to in response to what talk of the disbelievers this talk was said.

**Polytheism is Based on Desire:** 
Above, in verse 14, it is stated that I have been commanded to be the first one to submit and not to be among the polytheists. Following that, it is stated that in the matter of whether God has any partner or not, the greatest testimony can only be God's. The testimony of God which has been revealed to me in the form of the Quran is precisely this: He is One without partner. For this reason, I am not ready to assign any partner to Him contrary to this testimony. Here, the same announcement is repeated from a negative angle: I have been forbidden from the worship of those things which you call upon besides Allah. Here, "call upon" obviously refers to the calling which is of the nature of prayer and seeking help. In this way, calling upon anyone besides Allah is, in reality, His worship, and this thing is *shirk* (polytheism). Following this, it is stated: announce that I cannot follow your desires. "Desires" (*ahwa'*) refers to polytheistic innovations, and by describing them with the word *ahwa'*, the Quran has clarified their baselessness—that neither is there any testimony for their being God's partners present within intellect and nature, nor in God's word and revelation; you have forged these things purely on your own, and since the hope of their imaginary intercession has made you indifferent to all responsibilities of faith, action, and thought of the Hereafter, for this reason, these are highly pleasing to your inner self. In any case, they may be pleasing to you, but there is a big difference between truth and desire. It is impossible for me to ignore the truth and follow your false hopes, invalid desires, and baseless innovations. It is stated: if I do this, I will stray from the path of guidance and will never again become one who finds the path of guidance. The meaning is: first, the misguidance of polytheism is such that a person deviates from the straight path and gets lost in such crooked paths that it becomes impossible for him to come onto the true path. Second, after God's explicit prohibition, if I committed this error, then who is there whose guidance would become my support and who would take my hand and bring me onto the right path?

*Bayyina* refers to the Holy Quran, which Allah sent down as a testimony from His side that He has no partner or associate. The mention of this testimony has passed above in verse 19. Ahead, in verse 157, the same word is used for the Quran: *Aw taqooloo law anna unzila 'alayna al-kitabu lakunna ahda minhum faqad ja'akum bayyinatun mir-rabbikum wa hudan wa rahmah* (Or you say, "If only the Scripture had been revealed to us, we would have been better guided than they." So there has come to you a clear evidence from your Lord and guidance and mercy). 

*Wa kaththabtum bihi*—the referent of the pronoun is *bayyina*, but it is in view of the meaning, which is why the pronoun has come in the masculine form. There are many examples of this style in the Quran, where the pronoun is used not according to the outward form of the word but according to its meaning. Allama Ibn al-Qayyim has discussed this style in detail in his books. We will discuss its eloquence at appropriate places. Using the word *bayyina* for the Quran here has clarified its being a decisive argument (*hujjat-e-qati'ah*) and a clear testimony (*shahadat-e-wazihah*), and then the masculine pronoun has been used for it to also clarify that it refers to the Quran.

**The Quran is a Decisive Argument:** 
The meaning of the verse is that you are merely shooting arrows in the air and worshipping your desires. You possess no argument in favor of these imaginary deities of yours by which you can prove that God made them His partners. In contrast, I am upon an argument, a proof, and a decisive testimony from God, and I am presenting this very thing before you. God Himself has sent down this *bayyina* upon me, and it is so clear that there is no room for its denial, yet you deny it; and instead of understanding and believing it, you demand from me that I show you the punishment of God, so that you might acknowledge the truth of this Book.

*Ma 'indi ma tasta'jiloona bihi*—the punishment for which you are making haste is not with me. Deciding this matter is in the authority of God alone; all I can say is that He will clarify the truth and decide this dispute, and He will decide in a very excellent manner; He is the best of judges. It is stated: tell them that if the thing for which you are making haste were in my authority, the dispute between me and you would have been decided. *Wallahu a'lamu bith-thalimeen* (And Allah is most knowing of the wrongdoers) has two aspects. First, there is a threat to these disbelievers that God is well aware of these wrongdoers, so He will deal with them just as they deserve. The second aspect within it is that of delegation (*tafweez*)—that God is well aware of these wrongdoers, so leave their matter to Him. Here, "wrongdoing" (*thulm*) refers to wrongdoing against their own souls. The meaning is that these people are themselves wrongdoers against their own souls. God is giving them His mercy, but they are seeking His punishment. They are being given bread, but they are in pursuit of catching snakes.

*Mafatih* is the plural of *miftah*, which means key. That is, the keys to the treasures of the Unseen are with Him; besides Him, no one has knowledge of them. The same subject is stated in different words: *Lahu maqaleedus-samawati wal-ardi yabsutur-rizqa liman yasha'u wa yaqdir* (To Him belong the keys of the heavens and the earth; He extends provision for whom He wills and restricts [it]) (Ash-Shura: 12). *Bar* (land), *bahr* (sea), and *ratb* (moist) and *yabis* (dry), etc., express the content of encompassing; that is, the knowledge of God encompasses everything. *Thulumatil-ard* (darknesses of the earth) refers to the layers of the earth and its strata.

**The Mutual Connection Between Divine Knowledge and Tawheed/Hereafter:** 
This is further expansion of the preceding subject. When it was stated that the thing for which you are making haste is not in my authority, the authority of decision is only with God, then a highly appropriate occasion arose to express the vastness of divine knowledge and His encompassing. The belief in this vastness of Allah's knowledge is what creates the courage for complete delegation, complete trust, and complete contentment with destiny within the believers. Indifference to this even a little opens the paths of polytheism. This is the foundation of true faith in the Hereafter, and the correct perception and remembrance of it creates within man that fear which prompts him to adopt the correct path in life. From this statement of the Quran, the views of those who believe God has knowledge only of universals (*kulliyat*) are also entirely refuted. God has knowledge not only of universals but also of all particulars (*juz'iyat*). Every leaf that falls from a tree, every grain that is buried in the layers of the earth—all is in His knowledge, and every dry and moist thing is written in His register; and this register is not one in which He has to search for anything, but rather everything within it is perfectly clear at every moment. This is an expression of Allah's encompassing knowledge.

**Allegorical Observation of Death, Barzakh, and Gathering:** 
This statement of divine knowledge has created an occasion for the reminder of life after death, life after that, and accountability and reward/punishment—as if one matter has emerged from another. It should be kept in mind here that regarding the Hereafter, a great delusion arises for people from the loss of the correct concept of divine knowledge. A short-sighted human thinks that how can someone have knowledge of every single particular of the dying and living of such countless creation and of their every word and deed, so that He may resurrect everyone after death, gather them, and then sit down to account for them? The Quran has removed this impossibility and delusion here. First, it stated that the same God who knows every leaf falling from a tree and every grain buried in the earth will raise you after death and will inform you of whatever you did in this world. The introduction to this matter is raised in such a way that this dying and being raised again after death and being informed of all one's deeds is not a very far-fetched thing; rather, it is a reality whose rehearsal is happening before your own eyes every day and night. Every night, God causes you to die, and when He causes you to die, He knows everything you did during the day. Then, the next day, God raises you, and in this way, that term of life and fixed term which God has set for each of you is fulfilled. As if life, death, *Barzakh* (the interval), and being raised after death—the allegorical observation of this is being shown to each one of you every day; God has made this world in such a way that the rotation of His night and day is teaching all those truths for which you are being called, provided you possess eyes to see and hearts to understand. The Quran has here expressed sleeping every night as *wafat* (death) and waking up every morning as *ba'th* (resurrection), drawing attention to the fact that dying and being raised is happening every day; your dying and rising is just like your sleeping and waking, and just as God is not unaware of the deeds of any of your days when you sleep, similarly, when you sleep the sleep of death, God will not forget the deeds of your life; and just as after the sleep of every night of yours, there is a morning and you wake up rubbing your eyes, similarly, after the sleep of death, the morning of Resurrection will come, and you will feel as if all this was a matter of a morning and evening.

The meaning of the word *qahr* has been stated under verse 18, and *tafreet* under verse 38. *Hafathah* is the plural of *hafiz*, which means the person responsible for the guarding of something. From this, it refers to those divine guards who are appointed equally by God over every soul. The meaning is: no one should think that God is ever indifferent to any individual of His creation or any sheep of His flock; all are in His control at every moment. He keeps His guardian angels appointed over them at all times, who do not remain indifferent to their guarding for even a moment. Then, when the time of someone's death comes, it is stated: "Our messenger angels take his soul, and they do not fail in this duty." Neither can anyone escape their grasp, nor can they forget anyone, nor can anyone's death be advanced or delayed by even a single moment.

*Thumma ruddoo ilallahi mawlahumul-haqq*—then all will be returned to Allah, who is the true Master of all. That is, after death, everyone must encounter God Himself because He alone is the true Master. The other partners and intercessors people have forged will be of no use. *Ala lahul-hukm*—listen carefully, on that day, all authority for decision will be in His hand alone. Without His permission, none will be able to open their mouth before Him. *Wa huwa asra'ul-hasibeen*—that is, do not think that Allah will face any difficulty in this long account of all of divinity, or that time will be spent in it. He will place the entire account before everyone in an instant. His "Clear Book" (*Kitab-e-Mubeen*) will place before Him the entire record of every small and big deed of every person.

*Qul man yunajjeekum min thulumatil-barri wal-bahr*. *Thulumat* (darknesses) is an expression for calamities and disasters. The Quran has described the darkness of the sea in Surah An-Nur: "Or like darknesses within an unfathomable sea which is covered by waves, upon which are waves, over which are clouds—darkness, some of which is above some; when one puts out his hand [therein], he can hardly see it." Humans face similar storms and calamities on land as well.

*Tad'unahu tadarru'an wa khufyah*. *Tad'unahu* is in the state of *hal* (circumstantial accusative) for the pronoun of the object in *yunajjeekum*. The investigation of the word has passed under verse 44. Here, it is accompanied by the word *khufyah*, which means secretly. For this reason, *tadarru'* will mean to pray and beseech with weeping and wailing. *La'in anjana...* is the statement of this very prayer that exists in the heart and on the tongue of every person in such conditions.

In these verses, one psychological disease of man has been unveiled, and at the same time, an irrefutable psychological argument for Tawheed has been indicated. The context of the discourse is the same as the refutation of the demand for punishment which has been running from above. The meaning is: the state of man is strange—that when he is caught in some affliction, then he calls upon God, both wailingly and secretly in his heart, and pledges that if God saves him from this affliction, he will then live as His grateful and obedient servant. But when He saves him from it, he adopts the same life of ingratitude and disobedience in which he was involved before, and begins to think that now he has stepped outside the grasp of God and can never again fall into the grasp of God. So much so that if he is warned of God's grasp, he becomes stubborn and demands punishment, saying: show me the punishment, then I will believe. It is stated: ask them that when you get caught in some affliction on land or sea, who saves you from that whirlpool of affliction while you call upon God Himself, wailingly and secretly, and pledge that if God saves us from the affliction, we will live as His grateful servants? Then, make them answer themselves that it is God Himself who saves you from that affliction and saves you from all other calamities, whether they are small or large, and whether you call upon Him for them or not—but your state is that after this, you begin to associate partners [with Him] again.

Two things are to be kept in view here especially. One is the psychological argument for Tawheed that has been stated implicitly: that when man gets caught in some severe affliction, he turns only toward God; all other supports lose their reality in his sight. This is proof that only the belief in God is ingrained within the depth of man's nature; other things are merely fabricated, which fly away like false polish after passing through the furnace of trial. This argument has been stated in the Quran in various styles and in highly effective parables; we have clarified it in complete detail in our book, *Haqeeqat-e-Tawheed* (The Reality of Monotheism), under an independent title. Second, here gratitude and polytheism have been placed as two opposite things, from which it is understood that polytheism is not only that a person worships idols, but that arrogance, in which man begins to consider the blessings of Allah as the fruit of his own merit and the result of his own planning and capability, and then in the intoxication of pride, acts arrogantly, treads haughtily, and begins to do whatever he wants—this state is the opposite of gratitude, and whoever gets infected with this disease begins to consider himself a partner in divinity; the Quran has drawn the picture of this mentality in Surah Al-Kahf: "And present to them an example of two men: We granted to one of them two gardens of grapevines, and We have bordered them with palm trees and placed between them [grown] crops. Each of the two gardens brought forth its fruit and did not withhold anything therefrom. And We caused to gush forth within them a river. And he had fruit, so he said to his companion while he was conversing with him, 'I am greater than you in wealth and mightier in [numbers of] men.' And he entered his garden while he was wronging himself. He said, 'I do not think that this will perish—ever. And I do not think the Hour will occur. And even if I should be brought back to my Lord, I will surely find better than this as a return.' His companion said to him while he was conversing with him, 'Have you disbelieved in He who created you from dust and then from a sperm-drop and then proportioned you [as] a man? But as for me, He is Allah, my Lord, and I do not associate with my Lord anyone.'"

*Qul huwal-qadir...* (Say, "He is the [one] capable... "). That is, do not think that if you have attained a life of peace and satisfaction today, then you can never fall into God's grasp. God can rain stones upon you whenever He wills, send lightning, send whirlwinds. Similarly, whenever He wills, He can send an earthquake, flood, or any other earthly calamity from right beneath your feet. If not this, He can make tribe against tribe, group against group, and nation against nation among you clash with one another and make you taste the wrong and tyranny of one another. Every one of these things is within God's power at every moment. It is stated: see how We present the signs of Our power and the arguments of Our authority and control in various styles so that they may understand, but they wish to see Our punishment instead of understanding.

*Wa kaththaba bihi qawmuka wa huwal-haqq* (But your people have denied it, while it is the truth). The punishment with which the Quran has warned them is a reality and is to come to pass, but your nation has denied it. Now leave their matter to Us and tell them: *Lastu 'alaykum bi-wakeel* (I am not made a guardian over you)—meaning I have not been sent as an overseer over you so that I must necessarily drive you onto the path of faith and Islam, otherwise I would be questioned. Upon me, the responsibility was only of warning and propagation, which I have fulfilled. I have absolved myself of my responsibility. Now, if you are desperate for punishment, then *likulli naba'in mustaqarr* (for every important event, there is a fixed time). When that fixed time arrives, you will see it yourself. 

*Wa kaththaba bihi qawmuka*—the referent of the pronoun could be the punishment mentioned in verse 58, and it could also be the Quran, which is giving the news of this punishment and to which an indication passed in verse 57.

*Likulli naba'in mustaqarr*. *Naba'* refers to news of an important event. Here, it refers to the news of the punishment which the Quran is giving. *Mustaqarr* can be in the meaning of "place of settling" or "time of settling." We have taken it in the meaning of time of settling.

**The Upcoming Subject (Verses 68 to 73):** 
Ahead, the same theme that has been running from above is being stated in new styles and from new aspects. First, it is announced through the tongue of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) and the Muslims that there is no need to pursue those stubborn and rigid people who do not wish to listen; when you see that these people have turned to perverse argumentation and mockery, then leave them and separate yourself; only present any matter before them when they appear in a mood to listen and understand. Your responsibility is limited to propagation and reminder. You are not responsible for their faith, to such a degree that you must force faith into their hearts; do not care for their new demands either. Just fulfill the duty of reminder through this very Quran, so that whoever wishes to reform may reform and not be destroyed in recompense for their deeds. Before God, neither will anyone's support or intercession be of any use, nor will there be any room to offer compensation. 

Following this, it is announced through the tongue of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) that after having received Allah's guidance, we are not prepared to fall into the bewilderment and wandering of polytheism. We have been commanded to surrender ourselves to Allah, the Lord of the worlds, to perform His prayer, and to keep fearing Him; we have surrendered ourselves to Him; whoever wishes, let him choose this path, or else wander wherever he wishes. The meaning is that now there is no room for any dispute in this matter; we clearly announce our religion and path. Finally, an indication is given toward the truthfulness (*bil-haqq*) of this cosmic factory, from which it is intended to tell that Resurrection must come; on that day, the decision will be with God alone. He is the Wise and the Knowing. On that day, the truth of truth and the falsehood of falsehood will become clear to all, so if they do not listen, leave their matter to that day. In this light, read the upcoming verses.

**The Meaning of 'Khawd':** 
*Khawd* means entering into something. *Khadal-ma'a* means he entered the water. From this, the idiom *Khawd fil-hadith* was coined, which means extracting talk from talk, splitting hairs, and attempting to create new aspects of objection, criticism, and stubborn argumentation in a matter. This word is used in several places in the Quran, and in every place, it is used for that kind of excessive talk which aims to turn a matter into jest, fun, and mockery. For example, *Wa la'in sa'altahum layaqoolunna innama kunna nakhoodu wa nal'ab* (And if you ask them, they will surely say, "We were only conversing and playing"). Here, the reality of this word is opened in the following verse, *Wa tharil-latheena ittakhathoo deenahum la'iban wa lahwa*, because it refers to the same people who perform *khawd* in the signs of Allah. 

**Address, Though in the Singular, is General:** 
Although the address here is in the singular form, the dominant context is that the address is to the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), but this address is to all Muslims through the Prophet. As the discourse progresses, it has been clarified implicitly that the address is general. Then, in the following verse, the statement *Wa ma 'alallatheena yattaqoona min hisabihim min shay'* (And those who fear Allah are not held accountable for them at all) clarified the aspect of this address—that it is a general address to all Muslims. Then, in the aforementioned verse of Surah Al-Nisa, it has been stated in clear words that this address is general, because there, based on this very instruction of Surah Al-An'am, accountability was taken from those who violated this command by participating in the gatherings of the enemies of Islam where the signs of Allah were openly mocked.

**Two Aspects of This Instruction:** 
This instruction holds two aspects, and both are highly important. One is that this attitude is contrary to that wisdom of invitation which Allah has liked for the propagation of His religion. When a group or someone is filled with the fever of opposing and ridiculing something, presenting that thing before them in that very state is equivalent to increasing the severity of their fever and delirium. If a healer desires an increase in the patient's illness, then he is free to do whatever he wants; but no kind physician who desires the patient's health will ever make such a mistake. Keeping this consideration of circumstances in view, Muslims were instructed here that when you see that these opponents of Islam are bent upon mocking the Quran, have prepared the quiver of satire and contempt, and have rolled up their sleeves for opposition, then turn away at that time and wait for some time when this crisis-state has subsided a little, and then attempt to make them listen and understand. 

Second, this is contrary to the sense of honor for the truth that exists or should exist within the believers. If some person or group openly spews nonsense against God and His Messenger, then fighting with them is wrong for a caller, as mentioned above, and remaining silent is also wrong, because this injures the sense of honor for the truth which is a sign of faith, and whose weakness eventually involves a person in that hypocrisy wherein after becoming involved, he sees the insult and humiliation of God, Messenger, Quran, and Shariah with his own eyes and hears with his own ears, but he is so snake-bitten that he does not have the courage to open his mouth. 

Both these aspects are kept in view within this instruction here. The first is clear from the context itself, and the second was clarified by the Quran in the aforementioned verse of Surah Al-Nisa, because by citing this very instruction of Surah Al-An'am, it held the hypocrites accountable for participating in those gatherings of the opponents of Islam where the signs of Allah were openly mocked, even though they had been warned against this in the Quran.

*Wa imma yunsiyannakash-shaytanu fala taq'ud ba'dath-thikra ma'al-qawmith-thalimeen* (And if Satan should cause you to forget, then do not remain after the reminder with the wrongdoing people)—this is a further emphasis on the above instruction. The meaning is: if ever Satan causes you to forget, then after remembering, do not remain with such wrongdoers. This emphasis was necessary because sometimes a person arrives at a gathering, and there the talk keeps progressing until it reaches the mockery of religion and Shariah. At such a time, a person does feel that this is no longer a place to sit, but thinks: how to get up and leave such a gathering? Or if he is of the disputing type, he thinks: how to leave the field and step away? Both these thoughts are fitnah for a person. If the consideration of the gathering is a barrier, then this is evidence of the lack of sense of honor. A person should think: if someone were to insult his father and mother to his face, would he tolerate it in silence? The right of God and His Shariah is greater than that of parents, indeed greater than the entire world. And if he stays there for argument and debate, even though his intention is establishing truth and refuting falsehood, when the minds of those people have become corrupt and making them listen is equivalent to inciting them further, then engaging with such people will remain nothing more than a fight of ego. The purpose of truth will not only not attain any strength from it, but on the contrary, it will suffer severe loss. For this reason, the correct way is that a person should consider this Satan's trick and get up and leave such a gathering. 

It should be kept in view here that the correct meaning of *wa imma yunsiyannakash-shaytanu* is that if Satan ever traps you into such a situation or brings you to face such wrongdoers, then you are instructed to adopt this attitude. From this, it automatically follows that first, a person should remain alert so that Satan does not trap him into such a fitnah, but if he does trap him by causing him to forget Allah's guidance, then as soon as he realizes, a person should say *salam* to such a gathering and walk away, because those who mock the signs of Allah are the ultimate wrongdoers against their own souls, and their company can involve one in God's unknown wrath.

The letter *lam* in *liyaqooloo* is *lam-e-'aqibat* (of consequence), which does not express the cause but the result.

**Riches and Poverty in This World are for Testing:** 
This is an established law of Allah that We have given someone wealth in this world not because he deserved it. Similarly, if someone has been given poverty, it is not because he deserved it; rather, it is to test both by giving wealth to one and poverty to another. He gives wealth and position to whom He gives so that He may see whether they become grateful, humble, and obedient servants of His by obtaining the blessed favor of Allah, or become arrogant and haughty, becoming those who show off, cast away the poor, and sit as monopolists of divine blessings. Similarly, to whom He gives poverty, He gives so that He may see whether they remain patient in their poverty, contented with attained blessings and their own bread, satisfied with their destiny, and self-respecting in their poverty, or become hopeless and broken-hearted, low-spirited, spiritless, complaining of destiny, angry with God, and become wretched and humiliated. It is stated: We have placed them in this test. We granted them from Our favor so that they might become Our grateful servants. But due to their lack of understanding, Our favor became a fitnah for them. They presumed that they alone are the birthright owners of all blessings and the ancestral monopolists of all honor and position. The result of this lack of understanding was that now they began saying: if this Islam held any merit, would God have found only these low-class, wretched, and destitute people to honor with it? After all, where did we, the nobles, the leaders of Quraysh, and the chiefs of Ta'if die, that this blessing descended and it could not find our address and descended upon them? The Quran has referred to this pride of the leaders of Quraysh in other places as well; for example: *Wa qalal-latheena kafaroo law kana khayran ma sabaqoona ilayhi* (And those who disbelieved said about the believers, "If it had been a good thing, they would not have preceded us to it") (Al-Ahqaf: 11). Elsewhere: *Wa la aqoolu lillatheena tazdaree a'yunukum lan yu'tiyahumullahu khayran* (And I do not say to those whom your eyes hold in contempt, "Never will Allah give them any good") (Hud: 31). Faith and Islam aside, they considered even prophethood their monopoly and openly said that if God were to make someone a prophet, He would have made some chief of Mecca or Ta'if, for this office, only these (Muhammad, peace be upon him) were found.

**The True Recipients of the Blessing of Religion:** 
In response to this rebellion of theirs, the Quran stated: *Alaysallahu bi-a'lama bish-shakireen* (Is Allah not most knowing of those who are grateful?). The meaning is that the religion of God is not gold and silver, silk and velvet, whose saddles and trappings can be seen even on donkeys and mules, horses and camels. This is a heavenly blessing and divine mercy which is only the share of those who remained grateful to their Lord in every condition, those who appreciated the blessings of God, those who fulfilled the right of their abilities, those who kept their ears open, those who did not bandage their eyes with pride, and those who did not let their hearts die. Those wretched and ungrateful people who used all the outward and inward blessings of God against God Himself have no share in this blessing. The world is attained by both the good and the bad, but the blessing of religion is attained only by those who are grateful to God.

*Salamun 'alaykum* is a greeting, just as it is a word for parting; it is also a word for welcome. For example, angels, while welcoming those who remain steadfast on monotheism, will say: *Salamun 'alaykum udkhulul-jannata bima kuntum ta'maloon* (Peace be upon you, enter Paradise because of what you used to do).

**Instruction to Welcome the Poor Muslims:** 
Now, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is instructed to adopt an attitude exactly opposite to that which the leaders of Quraysh demanded. The leaders of Quraysh, as stated, wanted Muhammad (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) to shove away these people from himself, but Allah, in contrast, instructed the Prophet that when these servants of Ours who believe in Our signs come to you, you welcome them with the prayer of peace and mercy, and bring them the glad tidings on Our behalf that Allah has made mercy upon Himself for His faithful servants. He will surely bless you with His mercy. If any mistake is committed by any of you out of ignorance and then he repents and reforms, then Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.

**The True Yearning of the Believers:** 
Here, the glad tidings that Allah has given are only of mercy and forgiveness. There is no trace of any worldly wealth and position along with it. This sheds light on the inner self of those poor but world-and-world-possessions-indifferent servants of Allah. It shows that despite being deprived of worldly means, they held no longing for this world in their hearts. If they had a longing in their hearts, it was that they attain the pleasure of their Creator and Master. Allah blessed them with the very thing they were most desirous of, in accordance with this inward longing of theirs. From this, it is also indicated that the things for which the worshippers of the world die, in the eyes of the faithful servants of God, they do not have the value of a gnat's wing, neither in this world nor in the Hereafter.

*Qul anad'oo...* (Say, "Shall we call upon...?") *Ba'da ith hadanallahu* (After Allah has guided us). 

**God's Guidance Leaves No Excuse for Anyone:** 
Now, through the tongue of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), an announcement has been made that these people who are exerting all their power to drag you back into the same misguidance from which God has saved you—tell them clearly: do you wish that we call upon things besides Allah that have no power to benefit us nor power to harm us, and in this way, turn back on our heels into the same misguidance from which God has granted us the grace to emerge and guided us? The meaning is that if we were involved in this misguidance before, there was some excuse for it, but now if we choose to return, what excuse will we have? In *ma la yanfa'una wa la yadurruna*, the word *ma* for those deities is evidence of their contempt, and the negation of benefit and harm is in consideration of the original reality, because if benefit or harm reaches from any of these things, it reaches by the command of Allah, not that any of these things possess benefit or harm by themselves or by their own authority. *Ba'da ith hadanallahu* is a highly effective piece of advice for the addressees: even after the rising of this sun of guidance, those who are stumbling themselves and wish to cause others to stumble—let them reflect on their end.

*Kallathe-stahwathu-sh-shayatinu fil-ardi hayran...* (Like one whom the devils have enticed [to stray] upon the earth, confused...). *Istihwa'* means to lose one's mind, to lose one's wits, to leave one confused and helpless. *Ard* usually means earth, but here, the parable of the loss of path is being stated, which is why the context clarifies that it refers to the desert and wilderness, where there is a danger of losing the path. The attribution of the verb *istihwa'* to Satan is based on the meaning contained in the parable. This parable is of moral/spiritual loss, in which the role of the devils of jinn and men is greater. For this reason, by attributing the verb to Satan, the original truth has been indicated. *Huda* means straight path. 

**A Parable of the Opponents of Islam:** 
*Wa nuraddu 'ala a'qabina ba'da ith hadanallahu* is the parable of the guidance. The meaning is: after having found the highway of divine guidance, if we followed your word and turned back, involving ourselves again in the polytheism from which God saved us, then our example would be exactly like that person whom the devils have led astray in some wilderness, he is wandering about in confusion, his companions are calling out to him: "The path is this way, come to us," but his intellect is so lost that he cannot understand where to go. If the style of this parable appears to be a general parable of others, reflection shows that it is, in reality, the parable of these opponents of Islam who are under discussion here. In this parable, the matter is placed before them that you are like a traveler wandering in the wilderness; Satan and misguided leaders have destroyed your wits, and your rightly-guided companions (pointing toward the believers) are calling you toward the original path, but you are not listening to their call. See with what excellence their entire picture has been placed before them. The story is apparently of others, but the talk is entirely about them. *Qul inna hudallahi huwal-huda* (Say, "Indeed, the guidance of Allah—[it is] the [only] guidance"). That is, tell them that the original guidance is the guidance of Allah, so after having found the guidance of Allah, how can we follow any other method? 

*Wa umirna linuslima li-rabbil-'alameen* (And we have been commanded to submit to the Lord of the worlds). This is the answer to the polytheists' call to polytheism: you are inviting us to worship things that neither benefit us nor harm us, and we have been commanded to bow down and surrender before the Lord of the worlds. Here, the *lam* in *li-nuslima* expresses the purpose of the command, and the attribute *Rabbil-'alameen* (Lord of the worlds) is the argument for this emphasized command. The meaning is that Allah alone is the Lord of all the worlds, so who else can be worthy of surrendering one's self to?

*Wa an aqeemus-salata wattaqoohu wa huwallathe ilayhi tuhsharoon*. This is under *wa umirna li-nuslima li-rabbil-'alameen* and is its practical picture, but the style is changed from third person to second person, creating emphasis of direct address within it. Prayer is mentioned here as the first practical manifestation of that submission mentioned in *wa umirna li-nuslima li-rabbil-'alameen*. Piety (*taqwa*) here is in the meaning of adhering to all those limits God has commanded to be adhered to. *Ilayhi tuhsharoon* (to Him you will be gathered) gathers both the Hereafter and Tawheed, and this is the argument for the above commands—that establishing prayer and respecting the divine limits is necessary because one day one has to appear before God, and one has to appear before Him alone; on that day, there will be no other resort or master.

*Wa huwallathe khalaqas-samawati wal-arda...* (And it is He who created the heavens and the earth...). This is a highly comprehensive verse in which the argument for every part of the above segment, *Huwallathe ilayhi tuhsharoon*, is stated in highly concise words.

**The Necessary Consequence of the 'Haqq' (Truth) of the Cosmic Factory:** 
The signs of the Creator's power, wisdom, and providence present in the heavens and the earth testify to the truth that this factory is not the game of a player who made it merely to amuse himself, but it is a world made by an All-Powerful, All-Knowing, All-Wise, and Most Merciful and Compassionate Being. If it kept running like this—the oppression within it having no day for justice, the justice within it having no time for recompense, the wicked, evil, and corrupt within it receiving no punishment, the righteous, truth-seeking, and justice-upholding receiving no reward for their righteousness—then it means this entire factory is entirely aimless, purposeless, and false, whose maker considers no difference between good and evil, oppression and justice, truth and falsehood. The intellect and nature of man can in no way accept this. For this reason, the existence of a Creator in every corner of whose creation the signs of His wisdom, power, mercy, and providence are present, and present in such abundance that man can in no way encompass them—regarding Him, how can a person believe that He has no concern for our virtue and vice and our justice and oppression? If there is concern, and surely there is concern—for if this concern were not there, this world would become nothing more than a game, and a highly tyrannical game at that—then it necessarily follows that a Day of Justice should come for this world in which the complete mercy and complete wisdom of God may manifest, and every virtue may receive its reward and every evil its punishment.

*Wa yawma yaqoolu kun fayakoonu qawluhul-haqq* (And the day He says, "Be," and it is, His word is the truth). That is, no one should harbor the delusion that God will face any difficulty in bringing that day; He who created this world by His command "Be"—when He wishes to establish the Resurrection, He will establish it by the same command "Be." Ultimately, when He faced no difficulty in creating the world the first time, why would He face it again? *Qawluhul-haqq* (His word is the truth) means "whatever God says is to come to pass." No obstruction can arise in it even for a moment. *Wa lahul-mulku yawma yunfakhu fis-soor* (And His is the dominion the day the Horn is blown). That is, the day the Horn is blown for Resurrection, on that day, all authority and all kingdom will belong only to the One, the Dominant God. On that day, neither will anyone's power work, nor will anyone possess any authority, nor will anyone's effort and intercession be able to bring any benefit to anyone without His permission. All will be bowing before God; only His word will be decisive and enforced. *'Alimul-ghaybi wash-shahadah* (Knower of the Unseen and the manifest). He is the Knower of all the Unseen and the manifest, which is why neither will any apparent or hidden matter of anyone remain hidden from Him, nor will He be in need of asking anything from anyone, nor will anyone be able to add anything to His knowledge, nor will anyone be able to make any wrong excuse by misleading Him. *Wa huwal-hakeemul-khabeer* (And He is the Wise, the Knowing). The necessary requirement of this is that His every decision be based on justice, wisdom, knowledge, and awareness. Neither is there any defect in His justice and wisdom that He may make any falsehood truth or truth falsehood, nor is there any gap in His knowledge and awareness that He may fall into some delusion due to ignorance or someone may mislead Him and make truth falsehood or falsehood truth.

**Chapter 11: The Subject of Verses 74 to 90:** 
The discussion running from the beginning of the Surah has reached its point of climax here. Now, by referring to Prophet Abraham and all the prophets who came before and after him, it is stated that the call of all of them was the same as that which this Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is giving. Then, the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) is addressed and told that you must follow the guidance of this guided group in any case. If your nation does not listen to your words, then leave them to their state; your responsibility is only invitation and propagation. It is not your responsibility to land faith and guidance in their hearts. In this connection, the call to monotheism of Prophet Abraham (peace be upon him), which he gave to his father and his nation right at the beginning, is mentioned with special importance. The reason for the special mention of Prophet Abraham (peace be upon him) and his call, as we have explained in the commentary of Surah Al-Baqarah, is that both the Children of Ishmael and the Children of Israel acknowledged him as their ancestral patriarch and spiritual leader and were claimants that the religion they are upon, they inherited from him, and in all their polytheistic innovations, they presented his noble name as evidence. The mission of the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) was also upon this *Millat-e-Ibrahim* (Nation of Abraham), which is why it became necessary that just as it was clarified to the Children of Israel in Al-Baqarah and Ali 'Imran that the Judaism and Christianity they invented have no connection with the *Millat-e-Ibrahim*, similarly, it be clarified to the Children of Ishmael that the religion of polytheism they have adopted is their own invention; it has not even a distant connection with Prophet Abraham. Furthermore, the lessons the Prophet (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him) could receive from the call and every aspect of Prophet Abraham's life could not be received from any other method. The reason for this, as we indicated above, is that he came for the renewal and completion of that same *Millat-e-Bayda* (white/pure nation) whose call was given by Prophet Abraham and came among that nation which was the namesake of Prophet Abraham. In this light, read the upcoming verses:

**Difference of Opinion in Traditions Regarding the Name 'Azar':** 
*Azar* is the name of Prophet Abraham's father. In the Arabic and English translations of the Torah and the Talmud, its pronunciation is different from one another. The Quran has mentioned this name with such explicit detail that it appears that the difference of opinion in the traditions of the Jews regarding it is what it wishes to resolve, and since the Quran holds the status of a criterion for previous scriptures and is based directly on divine revelation, it must be believed that this name is the correct one.

**A Special Style of the Prophets' Call:** 
From the study of the religious literature of the Jews, it is known that *Azar* was not only a polytheist but also a maker and seller of idols; perhaps he was also a priest of the idol house. For Abraham to be born in the house of *Azar* and for this blow to be struck at the entire business of idolatry and idol-selling of the father at the hands of the son himself is a miracle of divine power. Among the signs of the truthfulness of the prophets is also this sign, that the truth they called the world to, they first gave its call to the ears of those who were nearest to them and also most dear to them.

Prophet Abraham (peace be upon him) gave a call to his father; there is only a brief indication of it here. In other places in the Quran, it is explicitly stated: "[Mention] when he said to his father, 'O my father, why do you worship that which does not hear and does not see and will not benefit you at all? O my father, indeed there has come to me of knowledge that which has not come to you, so follow me; I will guide you to an even path. O my father, do not worship Satan. Indeed Satan has ever been, to the Most Merciful, disobedient. O my father, indeed I fear that there will touch you a punishment from the Most Merciful so you would be to Satan a companion [in hellfire].' [His father] said, 'Have you no desire for my gods, O Abraham? If you do not desist, I will surely stone you, so avoid me a prolonged time.'" (Surah Maryam: 42-46). 

*A-tattakhithu asnaman aliha* (Do you take idols as deities?)—that is, idols fashioned by your own hands, and you made them deities! This is an open misguidance in which you are involved, and your nation too! Elsewhere, it is stated: *A-ta'budoona ma tan-hitoona* (Do you worship that which you [yourselves] carve?) (As-Saffat: 95).

*Wa kathalika nuree Ibraheema*, in other places, we have explained this style that this is, in reality, *kunna nuree Ibraheema* (*kunna* is omitted); according to the well-known style of the Arabic language, here *kunna* has been omitted.

**Investigation into the Word 'Malakut':** 
*Malakut* is like *Rahbut* from *Rahbah*, similarly *Malakut* is from *Mulk*. The lexical meaning of *Malakut* is honor and power, kingdom and sovereignty, but in the Quran, this word is used for that creational kingdom of God which is established over the heavens and the earth, indeed over everything. The invitation to reflect upon this *Malakut-e-Ilahi* (Divine Kingdom) has been given repeatedly in the Quran in different styles: "Do they not look into the kingdom of the heavens and the earth?" (Al-A'raf: 185). "Exalted is He in whose hand is the kingdom of all things, and to Him you will be returned."

**Reflection upon the Divine Kingdom is the Key to All Knowledge:** 
Here, it is being clarified that Prophet Abraham (peace be upon him) used to reflect upon the system of the heavens and the earth, and through the blessing of Allah's grace, Allah opened upon him those secrets and truths of His recognition which he clarified to his father and his nation. This truth should be remembered that it is from reflecting upon this *Malakut* that the key comes into hand by which the doors of correct thinking and correct action are opened; it is from this that the beginning of life is grasped, and it is from this that its destination is also known. Is there any Creator of this world, or did it appear by itself? If there is a Creator, is He One and Alone, or does He have other partners and associates? Will it be born and then end, or will it keep going on like this forever? If it has a Creator and Master, what are His attributes and characteristics, and for what purpose did He set up such a huge universe? Is there a criterion for truth and falsehood in this world, or is it a dark night? Is man responsible and accountable for his words and deeds, or entirely free and uncontrolled cattle? Is the same All-Powerful and Eternal's management and wisdom at work in the heavens and earth, or are separate wills and separate desires struggling for power within them? These and many other questions of this category are those on whose correct resolution is the foundation of correct thinking and correct action. For this reason, the Quran has invited to reflect upon this system of the heavens and the earth and has also guided our thinking in reaching correct results.

As far as reflection is concerned, reflection upon this *Malakut* is done by a scientist too, but he does all his reflection by making his own self or his limited environment the axis. His sight is only on his immediate benefit, which is why he does not try to reach those truths that distract his sight from his desired immediate benefit. He looks at a rose blooming in the garden with the sight that *gul-qand* (rose preserve) or some other thing can be prepared from it, from which such-and-such benefits can be obtained. After that, he does not fall into the trap of trying to see the manifestations of the Creator's power, craft, wisdom, mercy, and providence in the rose's beauty and elegance, its splendor and attraction, its fragrance and pleasurableness, and—becoming beside himself with these manifestations—drowning in the observation of the beauty and perfection of the One who made the rose, instead of stopping at the rose. *Ae gul to fakhar-e-san'am tu boo'e kase daree* (O rose, you are the pride of the craft, you possess the scent of someone). Whereas for a person of insight, this very aspect of the flower is more attractive. He thinks that if the purpose of the flower were only *gul-qand*, then what was the need for power to exhibit such generosity of flower-work on each and every petal? This flower-work and craftsmanship is performed precisely so that every petal of the flower serves as a register of the recognition of the Creator.

Newton saw an apple falling from an apple tree to the ground. From this, his mind shifted toward the principle of gravity. Then, many principles were discovered from this principle, which proved very useful in scientific research and discoveries, but his sight could not go beyond a specific limit. All the activities of this principle remained limited only within the narrow confines of this world. Otherwise, the question arises from here as well: who is the One who has bound each and every thing of the universe—whether small or big—with this law of attraction? Clearly, the honest answer to this question can only be that *Thalika taqdeerul-'azeezil-'aleem* (that is the measure of the Mighty, the Knowing). But because very heavy responsibilities fall upon man from this question and answer, our scientists flee from it.

**The High Sight of the Prophets:** 
In the verse under discussion, the statement of Allah that "And thus We showed Abraham the observation of the Divine Kingdom in the heavens and the earth" points to the same high sight which, through the blessing and guidance of Allah, is obtained especially by the prophets of Allah, and those people also receive a portion of this blessing according to their capability who reflect upon this universe faithfully and instead of fleeing from the results of their reflection, welcome them. *Wa yatafakkaruna fee khalqis-samawati wal-ardi rabbana ma khalaqta hatha batila* (And they reflect upon the creation of the heavens and the earth, "Our Lord, You did not create this aimlessly") points to the people of this group.

*Wa liyakoona minal-mooqineen*—here, in accordance with the well-known rule of the Arabic language, of which more than one example has passed, the conjoined-to (*ma'toof 'alayh*) is omitted, whose determination will be made by the context; here, clarification ahead is present that from this observation, Allah guided Prophet Abraham (peace be upon him) toward that argument of His monotheism which He established upon his nation. Thus, it is stated: *Wa tilka hujjatu-na atayhaha Ibraheema 'ala qawmihi* (And this is Our argument which We gave to Abraham against his nation). From this, it is clear that those who consider this the intellectual evolution of Prophet Abraham are entirely contrary to the Quran. This is not the evolution of Prophet Abraham's thought; if it can be called anything, it is the evolution of his call. If this had been the evolution of Prophet Abraham's thought, the words *'ala qawmihi* (against his nation) would not have been needed.

Here, keep a few more things in mind. First, the prophets (peace be upon them) are born on a sound nature and are raised on a sound nature. Even before prophethood, they never face doubt in the matter of monotheism and polytheism. Monotheism is the covenant of nature which God took from the children of Adam before sending them into the world, and it is proven from the Quran that on the basis of this covenant, every person will be responsible before Allah, whether the call of a prophet reached him or not. In such a state, there is no room to harbor the suspicion for any prophet that even before prophethood, he could have been polluted with any polytheism; even before prophethood, the prophets, as far as the principles of nature are concerned, are entirely on the *Fitratullah* (nature of Allah), because they are the best fruit of the tree of nature. In that era, the quest they possess is not for God but for God's pleasure and His commands. This quest is, in reality, the thirst of their sound nature which flares up at its maturity and is thirsty for satisfaction and comfort. This satisfaction they obtain from the *Abr-e-Naisan* (spring rain) of revelation. For the prophets, revelation is not the light in darkness, but light upon light: *Noorun 'ala noor, yahdillahu linurihi man yasha'u* (Light upon light; Allah guides to His light whom He wills). God willing, we will discuss this issue in detail in the commentary of Surah Al-Noor.

Second, Prophet Abraham's observation of the Divine Kingdom mentioned above is not from before prophethood but after being honored with prophethood, when he initiated his call. First, he gave the call to his father, and this has been the well-known *Sunnah* (practice) of the prophets of God that they gave the call to their nearest relatives first. After that, he turned toward his nation and announced the truth before them gradually in a manner whose details passed above. The evidence that this happened after prophethood is present in verses 79 to 80. Who except a prophet can say these manifest words—these living, eternal words?

Third, if this were the intellectual evolution of Prophet Abraham (peace and blessings of Allah be upon him), then the order of events would have to be considered entirely contrary to nature. After all, why did a small star draw his attention first? Where had the sun, which rises with such splendor every morning, gone? For this kind of unnatural observation, then even more unnatural is the tradition that people had to forge that Prophet Abraham was born in a cave, lived and grew up in it, and when he came out of it, it was night time, and the first thing he observed among the attractive things of this universe was the star Venus (*Zuhra*).

Fourth, in the deductive method of argumentation, if the speaker accepts some word of the opponent, he does not accept it to believe it, but wishes to defeat him with the very same blow which the opponent considers his special blow. Allah, in the Quran, has mentioned the *Sunnatullah* of *Istidraj* (gradual leading to ruin) for the deniers of the prophets, whose special characteristic is also that Allah seizes them from precisely where they pride themselves on their success and victory. If Prophet Abraham's nation worshipped the sun, moon, Venus, and Jupiter, then after all, what was their argument for their divinity? Nothing but that they rise and shine. Prophet Abraham grasped this very thing: if rising and shining is the proof of their divinity, then come, observe their setting and turning dark too, and tell me where that argument of yours went! The psychological point of humanity should also be kept in view here—that the best time to deliver the most effective speech on the instability and impermanence of those whose divinity is derived merely from their temporary splendor is when their corpse lies before their worshippers.

Fifth, in the methods, address, and argumentation of the prophets—the styles like *istidraj* (gradual leading), jest, satire, *tawriyah* (equivocation), and gradualism, etc., which are found here and there—all are based on the requirements of human nature. Each of these styles has a place, and each style has its own rank. Sometimes a pause, which is apparently a stop, covers thousands of stages of the journey, and a heart-attracting satire, which is apparently satire, becomes heavier than thousands of arguments. If God wills, the psychological aspects of the call of the prophets will be under discussion in the final group of the Quran; we will also express the correct meaning of some words there, due to whose incorrect understanding many people have become victims of highly regrettable misunderstandings.

*Falamma ra'al-qamara bazighan...* (And when he saw the moon rising...). Similarly, one day, in the spread-out moonlight of the full moon, he created an opportunity for this teaching again, and in the exact same tone and style as before, he said regarding the moon the same thing he said regarding the star. Then, when it too set, he said in the same manner, addressing himself and making others listen: *La'in lam yahdinee rabbee la-akoonanna minal-qawmid-dalleen* (If my Lord does not guide me, I will surely be among the straying people). Reflect: the step of teaching here is ahead compared to the previous one. Here, not only was this truth revealed that those who set are not worthy of worship, but it was also revealed that taking these setting things as gods is open misguidance. Also, this truth was revealed that this misguidance is no ordinary thing but a matter of great regret and sorrow. In addition, it was revealed that the source of guidance is God alone; if He does not grant guidance, man, taking everything shining for gold, runs after it. All these things Prophet Abraham (peace be upon him) said to himself, which is why there could be no reason for those who heard it to be angry; rather, whoever possessed even a little ability to reflect must have been lost in this thought: this person is so restless in the quest for guidance, and we are like stone, not prepared to move from our place.

Similarly, one day, he made the rising and setting of the sun the means of his teaching. When the sun came out with its splendor and reached the meridian, he said the same thing regarding the sun in the same style as before, which he said regarding the star and the moon. But he also added: *Hatha akbaru* (This is the greatest?). The context clearly indicates that he said this by way of satire, contempt, and mockery. But the hearers must have breathed a sigh of relief again that let it be, this is also a gain from this madman. He does not believe in Venus and the moon, so be it, but he acknowledges our great deity, the sun. But this satisfaction of theirs did not prove to be long-lasting either. Ultimately, the sun also had to set; it too set. When it set, Prophet Abraham (peace be upon him) announced the truth openly, addressing all: *Ya qawmi innee baree'un mimma tushrikoon* (O my people, I am disavowed from what you associate [with Allah]). Up until now, whatever he had said was in the nature of expressing his own impressions upon himself, although the purpose of it was indirectly to awaken the intellect and conscience of the nation as well, but he had not addressed the nation directly. Now, he addressed them directly and announced his disavowal of their religion and their deities.

*Innee wajjahtu wajhiya lillathee fataras-samawati wal-arda haneefan wa ma ana minal-mushrikeen* (Indeed, I have turned my face toward He who created the heavens and the earth, inclining toward truth, and I am not of the polytheists). This is the expression of that announcement of disavowal and its comprehensive word. The meaning is: I have cut myself off from all false deities and, becoming entirely oriented, turned my face toward that Lord who is the Creator of all heavens and earth. And I am not among the polytheists. The *lam* in *lillathee* is evidence that *wajjahtu* also contains the content of *aslamtu* (I have submitted). That is, I have surrendered my entire self to the Creator and Master of the heavens and the earth. This is the great verse of *Tawheed* and Islam, and the comprehensive word of the *Millat-e-Ibrahimiyyah* (Nation of Abraham), which is why we begin our prayers with this same truth.

**Response to the Threats of the Nation and the Answer of Prophet Abraham:** 
*Wa hajjahu qawmuhu*—when the matter reached here, Prophet Abraham had announced not only monotheism but also his disavowal of polytheism and polytheists, his nation stood up for argument and dispute against him, and since the foundation of polytheism, as we indicated, is entirely on fear and superstitious fears, the people of the nation began to threaten him in various ways: if you announce hatred and rebellion against the deities, you will fall into their grasp, you will become blind, you will become crippled, lightning will strike you, you will die a wretched death. Prophet Abraham (peace be upon him) answered: you dispute with me in the matter of God—that why do I believe in Him alone, why do I not assign partners to Him? This very God has granted me the guidance that He has no partner. It must be kept in view here that Prophet Abraham's (peace be upon him) nation was not a denier of God but associated partners with Him, and this matter is not unique to Prophet Abraham's nation; rather, no nation in the world has ever denied God—whoever stumbled, stumbled in the matter of *Tawheed*. 

*Wa la akhafu ma tushrikoona bihi illa an yasha'a rabbee shay'an* (And I do not fear what you associate with Him [and will not be harmed] unless my Lord should will something). This is the answer to the nation's threats: I do not fear these imaginary partners of yours in the least; they cannot cause me any harm, as long as my Lord does not wish to cause me any harm. Benefit and harm are in His authority alone; none has the authority without His permission to harm a single hair of mine. His knowledge encompasses everything; for this reason, I also do not fear that anyone can cause me any harm in His ignorance. What kind of things do you say? Do you people not think? *Illa an yasha'allahu* contains the content of delegation to God, which expresses the truth that whatever I am saying, I am saying on God's trust. This subject has also come in the tongue of Prophet Shu'aib (peace be upon him) in Surah Al-A'raf: "We would have fabricated against Allah a lie if we returned to your religion after Allah had saved us from it. And it is not for us to return to it, except that Allah, our Lord, should will. Our Lord has encompassed all things in knowledge. Upon Allah we have relied. Our Lord, decide between us and our people in truth, and You are the best of those who give decision" (Al-A'raf: 89). From this, it is known that the completion of even the purest intentions and truest resolves is dependent upon Allah's grace. For this reason, a servant should not say anything—even with the purest trust—with a claim; rather, he should say it on God's trust. This is because there are trials in every path, and to pass through these trials is impossible without His help and grace.

*Wa kayfa akhafu ma ashraktum* (And how should I fear what you associate [with Him]?). That is, you should fear the wrath and fury of God, for you have associated things as partners of God for which you have no document or argument revealed by God that He made them His partners, but you are the ones frightening me! As far as God is concerned, you believe in Him, I believe in Him; this is an accepted matter. Now, are there some others who are partners and associates of God? Then the burden of proof is upon you. If you have made them partners of God without proof, then you are criminals in the confession of God. And if He punishes you for this, then you deserve punishment. What crime have I committed for which I should fear? If you possess any knowledge, then tell me, who is the rightful one for the security of God: I or you? But this is a strange piece of irony that you, being the thieves, turn and scold the policeman.

*Allatheena amanoo wa lam yalbisu eemanahum bi-thulmin...* (They who believe and do not mix their belief with injustice...). *Thulm* refers to, as we have explained in more than one place, polytheism. Now, this is the statement of the original truth in the matter of monotheism: faith in God is only valid when it is clean from every trace of polytheism. You consider your partners as the guarantee of security and are indifferent to God, whereas those who rely on God alone in every matter and are free from polytheism are the rightful ones for security. These people alone are on guidance; every path besides this is a path of misguidance.

*Tilka hujjatu-na atayhaha Ibraheema 'ala qawmihi* (And this is Our argument which We gave to Abraham against his nation). This points toward the argument of monotheism that Prophet Abraham (peace be upon him) established upon his nation and which passed in detail above. Regarding it, it is stated that this is Our argument which We gave to Abraham to establish against his nation. From this, it is clear that those who consider this the intellectual evolution of Prophet Abraham are entirely contrary to the Quran.
